stacks.name: Startup (Complete)
stacks.description: Operations, finance, and growth tools to run your startup.

children.text: After testing 100+ tools, building our own startup, and investing in 30+ others, these are the tool that most founders use and love.

stacks.name: Startup Operations
stacks.description: The right setup turns customer feedback into product decisions and actually keeps everyone aligned.

children.text: Every founder hits this point.
Your inbox becomes your help desk. Slack turns into your task manager.
Every day feels like catch-up, marking messages unread just to remember who's still waiting on you.
We've tested hundreds of tools, building our own startup and investing in 30+ others, and I've seen this pattern over and over.
The problem isn’t your team, it’s the chaos. The right setup turns customer feedback into product decisions and actually keeps everyone aligned.
Let’s break down the tools that make it possible.

stacks.name: Startup Finance
stacks.description: Most founders think finance mistakes are just part of the journey, they're not.

children.text: You need a way to take payments, pay people, and track where every dollar’s going.
We’ve spoken with hundreds of founders, made the same mistakes ourselves, and finally figured out what actually works. Here's the finance stack we landed on.
Every founder dreams of hiring a great growth marketer.
But here’s the truth: even when you find that person, you might be the one slowing them down.
I’ve been that technical founder, building everything from our website to email flows in custom code, until every marketing tweak needed my attention.
After investing in 30+ startups and talking with hundreds of founders, I’ve realized it’s the same story everywhere...you need to get out of your marketing team's way.
Here are the tools that make it possible.

Superhuman simply helps make checking your emails less dreadful and more enjoyable. Every action, including loading, is nearly instantaneous (unlike Gmail). It has a minimal and beautifully designed interface, along with quick shortcut keys to get through your inbox more quickly. We've used Superhuman for 6+ years and definitely think it's worth it compared to using Gmail.
If you're unsure, grab a
Superhuman Mail free trial
(30–days free) for yourself if it's worth the time savings for you.

Mercury Bank has a more modern banking experience with top-notch QuickBooks Online integration, great UI, and a strong feature set, while Novo frustrated me with clunky integrations and felt limited for anything beyond very small businesses.
Only use Novo if you're a freelancer or solopreneur with a small account and can't use Mercury Bank for some reason.
We love Copper & folk! We recommend folk for teams who are new to using a CRM (perhaps have never used one before and want something super simple and quick to setup).
We recommend Copper for teams with more established processes who are looking to automate their processes and integrate their CRM with other third party software.

children.text: Viktor actively jumps in to help with your actual work in Slack, taking tasks off your plate before you even ask, while Sintra AI feels more like a simple idea generator for beginners.
Only use Sintra AI if you have zero experience and just want basic suggestions; choose Viktor for real business impact and team adoption.

Viktor actually takes work off your plate without you needing to spell everything out. It reads your Slack channels, picks up on what needs doing, and starts executing real tasks for you and your team, getting things right most of the time with barely any hand-holding. The experience is consistent across different roles, from marketing to engineering, and it remembers your preferences, so you're not constantly retraining it. The whole team adopted it fast and kept using it because it genuinely reduced their workload.

Sintra AI is incredibly easy for non-technical business owners to try, and its proactive "helpers" make it feel approachable, almost like a game. But the work it produces is mostly throwaway, with only a small fraction being usable, and it doesn't hold up for anyone running a serious business. The novelty fades quickly, and it even asks for personal business info that feels invasive.

If you actually want an AI agent to take on real tasks and make a dent in your workload, Viktor is in another league. Sintra is only worth considering if you want a low-stakes, playful intro to AI and don't care whether the output is useful. For anyone who needs real help, pick Viktor.

app.name: Viktor
app.description: AI Coworker for everyone else

children.text: Viktor immediately fits into Slack, proactively helps your team, and everyone wanted to use it right away, while OpenClaw is risky and only safe for hobbyists who do not care about privacy or security.
Only use OpenClaw if you are just experimenting on a spare device and do not mind security risks; otherwise, choose Viktor.

Viktor stands out for anyone who cares about security and control. It's SOC 2 compliant, gives you granular settings for access, and lets you restrict what it can do across your tools. You decide exactly what Viktor can touch, and there's a clear process for limiting its reach.

OpenClaw, on the other hand, is a red flag for anything important. Even people with deep security backgrounds say it's a huge risk, and there's no way to confidently lock it down. It might be a fun experiment for hobbyists who don't care about privacy, but it's not safe for business or sensitive work.
If you want an AI agent you can actually trust with your data and workflows, Viktor is the only real option here. OpenClaw just isn't safe for anything that matters.
Viktor jumps in on Slack, figures out what your team needs, and offers to handle work before you even ask, while Claude just helps with writing, coding, or research if you guide it.
Only use Claude if you want a helper for writing or brainstorming; stick with Viktor if you want an AI that actually takes work off your plate.

Viktor stands out for how quickly entire teams pick it up and start using it without any push or hand-holding. Once added to Slack, Viktor immediately reads your channels, introduces itself, and starts suggesting real, relevant tasks it can take off your plate. People across different roles just started using Viktor for their own needs, from strategy to engineering, and all of it happened organically. No one had to be convinced or trained. Adoption is almost instant, and the tool adapts to each person's workflow right away.

Claude, on the other hand, is good for individuals who want a writing or research partner, but it requires you to import your own briefs or skills before it starts feeling truly useful. Out of the box, most people feel underwhelmed until they do some upfront work to train it. Adoption is much slower and more manual, and even then, it's more of a solo tool than something the whole team naturally starts using together.

If you want something the whole team will actually start using right away, Viktor is in a different league. Claude is strong for deep writing and thinking work, especially for those willing to invest in setting it up, but it just doesn't have the same immediate, team-wide adoption or proactive engagement that Viktor brings. For rapid, organic adoption across a business, Viktor is the clear pick.
Viktor jumps in with suggestions on what to handle for you in Slack, while ChatGPT waits for you to ask it something.
Only use ChatGPT if you just want to ask questions, but use Viktor if you want your AI to notice things and actually help without prompting.

Viktor stands out for proactive task delegation. It doesn't wait for you to tell it what to do, it reads your Slack channels, figures out what's happening, and actually suggests specific things it can take off your plate. It learns your team's workflows without long explanations, remembers your preferences, and adapts to each person's role. You end up spending almost no time onboarding or explaining processes. The whole team picks it up fast because Viktor shows up with relevant, actionable help before anyone even asks.

ChatGPT is excellent for general memory and context in conversations, and it's super flexible across text, voice, and file types. But when it comes to actual delegation, you still need to direct it, set things up, and organize projects manually. It won't proactively spot work for you or execute tasks unless you prompt it. Its agent features are solid but don't go beyond what's already available in more specialized tools.

If you want an AI that truly takes work off your plate without hand-holding, especially for teams living in Slack, Viktor is the clear choice for proactive task delegation. ChatGPT is great as a thought partner and for handling complex or varied tasks, but it doesn't match Viktor's ability to jump in, adapt, and handle real delegation on its own.

children.text: Claude helps you get work done, write, and code, but if you're a small business owner, using it for building your own software will just frustrate your team and get dropped fast compared to Granola.
Only use Granola if you need an always-on meeting assistant that captures everything and becomes your go-to source for meeting records, otherwise stick with Claude.

Granola nails meeting recall and in-the-moment assistance because it's built for exactly that: always-on, invisible meeting memory without bots or awkwardness. If you want perfect recollection of what happened, the ability to ask questions mid-call, or a non-intrusive way to keep every meeting on record, Granola just works. It's the go-to for teams who treat meetings as a source of truth and need a lightweight, always-there assistant.

Claude, on the other hand, is all about helping with writing, research, and complex thinking tasks. It's not designed to listen in on meetings or act as a meeting repository. You won't get that persistent, searchable call memory or real-time meeting context from Claude.

So, if your priority is capturing, recalling, and working with meeting content, especially without clunky bots or making people self-conscious, Granola is the clear pick. Claude isn't trying to solve this problem, so it just can't compete here.

app.name: Granola
app.description: The AI notepad for people in back-to-back meetings

children.text: Granola records every meeting automatically and gives you instant recall plus live help during calls, while Littlebird tries to use more context from your calendar, email, and on-screen context, with an AI note taker, that's a bit less flushed out.
Only use Littlebird if you want your assistant to pull from more apps as context and specifically your screen, but for privacy-focused meeting and call recording, Granola is our top pick.

Granola stands out for anyone focused on meeting recording and note taking without the hassle or intrusiveness of bots. It works in the background for both in-person and virtual meetings, never gets kicked off a call, and doesn't require others to know it's running. You get instant AI-powered recall and can ask questions mid-call, making it a go-to for teams that want every meeting captured and accessible, no exceptions.

Littlebird, on the other hand, leans into broader context by pulling in not just meetings but your whole on-screen activity. If you want an assistant that tracks everything you do for deeper context, and you're fine with the privacy tradeoff, Littlebird unlocks a wider scope of assistance.

For pure meeting recording and note taking, especially where privacy and non-intrusiveness matter, Granola is the obvious pick. If you actually want your assistant to see everything you do for maximum context and you're comfortable with that, Littlebird brings more to the table. But for most teams wanting reliable, straightforward meeting coverage, Granola is simply less invasive and better focused on nailing the core job.

Motion actually feels like having a personal AI assistant that manages your team's tasks, schedules, and priorities for you, while Granola just helps you remember meetings and offers call context without needing a meeting bot.
Only use Granola if you need an always-on meeting memory and context tool, but pick Motion if you want AI to actively organize and drive your daily work.

Motion completely takes over your daily planning by auto-scheduling tasks onto your calendar, so you start every day with a clear, actionable plan. If you want to stop thinking about what to do next and just get to work, Motion actually eliminates manual planning and the constant shuffling of tasks. It's built for people who want to finish meaningful work, not just tinker with productivity setups or endlessly reorganize.

Granola, on the other hand, is all about meetings. It acts like an always-on, invisible note-taking assistant, giving you perfect recall of what happened in every meeting and letting you ask questions about the meeting context, even mid-call. It's lightweight, doesn't interrupt calls, and is basically your go-to for making meetings more useful and the details retrievable.

If your main pain point is getting your day mapped and tasks scheduled so you actually do the work, Motion is the obvious pick. But if you live in meetings and want a way to capture, recall, and interact with meeting content without bots or interruptions, Granola is the better fit. Each one crushes its own lane, but they solve totally different efficiency problems. Pick Motion if you want your whole workday planned with zero effort. Choose Granola if you need a flawless meeting memory and real-time meeting help.
Granola captures every meeting automatically and handles live questions mid-call, while Otter just records and transcribes when you hit record.
Only use Otter if you need quick personal recordings on the go; choose Granola for any business or team meeting use.

Granola stands out by acting as an always-on meeting assistant built specifically for business settings, covering all internal and external meetings without needing a disruptive bot to join calls. It provides real-time context and lets you interact with its AI mid-meeting, making it feel like a true participant that helps steer and document the conversation. The workflow is seamless and unobtrusive, so every meeting gets captured and indexed without anyone else even noticing.

Otter, on the other hand, is positioned as a straightforward transcription tool that shines for personal use, think recording a doctor's appointment or a casual chat. It's easy to use, just hit record and pocket your phone, but it doesn't offer the active, context-aware assistance or company-wide meeting repository that Granola brings to the table.

If you need a business-first solution that reliably captures and helps you work with your meetings, Granola is the clear pick. Otter is still handy for simple personal recordings, but it doesn't keep up when you need a professional meeting record or real-time help. For business meetings, Granola easily takes the lead.
Granola captures every meeting and brainstorming session automatically and lets you ask questions mid-call, while Gong focuses on analyzing sales calls and emails for large sales teams.
Only use Gong if you manage a big sales team and need extra deal insights; stick with Granola for all other meeting needs.

Granola stands out for effortless, always-on meeting capture that works even in person or during brainstorming, without needing a bot to join or alerting others. It's lightweight, can't be kicked off calls, and feels invisible, making it perfect for teams that want every meeting, remote or in person, documented and easily referenced. You get real-time AI help mid-call, with the ability to ask context-aware questions as the conversation unfolds, so it feels like a live assistant is there to help you keep up.

Gong is built for enterprise sales teams and focuses on analyzing sales interactions and surfacing deal risks, not just capturing meetings. Its recording feature is just the starting point for broader sales process insights, but it's not optimized for general meeting note-taking or seamless, invisible recording across all types of meetings.

If you want a meeting note taker that quietly captures everything without disrupting the flow or requiring others to know, Granola is the clear pick. Gong is strong for sales process analysis, but for pure meeting capture and live assistance, Granola is way more practical.
Granola gives you a full record of every meeting and live help during calls, while Dialpad Meetings just makes joining and integrating harder.
Only use Dialpad Meetings if you are fine with extra hassle and missing the meeting memory Granola provides.

Granola sidesteps all the user friction that plagues Dialpad Meetings. You never have to fight to get people to adopt a new platform or teach them where basic controls are. Granola just listens in quietly, no bots, no extra downloads, and nobody even needs to know it's there. That makes it effortless to capture meetings, whether they're in-person or remote, and you never run into resistance or confusion.

Dialpad Meetings, on the other hand, constantly battles user inertia. Its interface feels unfamiliar, people get lost looking for controls, and the lack of plug-and-play integrations just adds more hoops to jump through. If you want to connect it to your CRM, you have to rig up a whole separate meeting recorder tool, which means extra cost and a messier workflow.

If you care about adoption speed and not having to hassle your team or guests to change their habits, Granola is the clear pick. It just works in the background, no learning curve, no friction, and doesn't disrupt anyone's workflow. Dialpad Meetings might look clean, but getting actual buy-in is a headache that never really goes away.
Granola handles meetings by recording and recalling everything, while ChatGPT does not offer this meeting assistance or recall.
Only use ChatGPT if you do not need meeting memory, otherwise choose Granola for any meeting or brainstorming session.

Granola is purpose built for meetings and acts as an always-on assistant that captures every conversation, enabling perfect recall and real-time context-aware help during calls. It listens without requiring bots, works in both virtual and in-person settings, and lets you query past meetings instantly. If meeting memory, mid-call context, and seamless note taking are what you care about, Granola is the clear pick.

ChatGPT, on the other hand, is a general purpose AI you can use for just about anything, including coding help or answering questions. It's accessible to everyone and doesn't require technical skills, but it isn't designed to automatically capture meeting content or provide on-the-fly context during calls. If you need a meeting assistant, ChatGPT simply can't compete with Granola's specialized workflow.

So if your focus is meeting recall, context, and hands-off note taking, Granola outperforms ChatGPT by a mile. For anything outside meetings, ChatGPT is broader, but for meetings, Granola is what you want.
Granola records every meeting automatically and gives you perfect recall without needing a meeting bot, while tl;dv focuses on shared note taking and live collaboration during calls.
Only use tl;dv if you need everyone editing notes together live, otherwise Granola is the one to rely on for meeting records and answers.

Granola stands out because it's always on and invisible, no bots joining, no one else even needs to know it's running, and you get perfect recall for any meeting, including in-person and brainstorming sessions. There's no playback, but you can ask its AI Chatbot anything about past or current calls, even mid-meeting, and get context-rich answers or live suggestions to keep the conversation moving. This makes it feel like you've got an active research assistant in every meeting without any disruption or awkwardness.

tl;dv, on the other hand, is all about visible, real-time collaboration. You and your team type notes together in a floating pop-up, see each other's updates live, and everything gets timestamped and shared instantly. It's perfect if you want everyone involved in the note-taking and sharing process, especially with Slack integration, but it's more focused on digital meetings and doesn't offer that hidden, always-on presence or the AI-driven context support that Granola brings.

If you want invisible, comprehensive meeting capture that works everywhere and acts like a silent assistant, Granola is the clear pick. If live, shared note-taking during calls is your priority and you want everyone to contribute in real time, tl;dv is the stronger fit. For anything that relies on effortless, unobtrusive meeting recall and in-call AI support, Granola wins.
Granola gives you always-on meeting recall and help without needing a bot, while Fireflies joins meetings as a guest and can feel cluttered and buggy.
Only use Fireflies if you need advanced audio analysis and like sorting soundbites; stick with Granola for simple, reliable meeting capture.

Granola keeps things simple and invisible by recording all your meetings, even in-person ones, without needing a bot to join or anyone else to know it's running. You never have to worry about organizing or managing files after the call, and you can ask its AI for context or questions mid-meeting without breaking flow. It just quietly captures everything, so nothing gets lost, and you don't have to change how you run meetings. We go into depth as to why we use it over all the other
AI note takers
we've tried in our
Granola AI Review

Fireflies piles on features like soundbites, playlists, and deep integrations, but this comes at the cost of a cluttered experience that demands constant organizing. If you don't actively manage and tag your calls, it feels like you're missing out or letting things slip through the cracks. The interface is busy, and the pricing gets expensive quickly, especially if you want video recording or need to access older meetings.

If you want a note taker that just works in the background and makes sure every meeting is captured and easy to reference, Granola is the obvious choice. Fireflies only makes sense if you have a specialized workflow that relies on organizing and sharing snippets, but for most people, Granola's hands-off approach is far less hassle and makes it impossible to miss or forget a meeting detail.

items.name: Superhuman Mail
items.description: Get through your inbox 2x as fast (for teams of all sizes).

children.text: Superhuman Mail gets you through email way faster and stays minimal, while Front mixes email with social and chat in one inbox for teams.
Only use Front if your team must handle email, social, and chat messages together every day; otherwise, stick with Superhuman Mail.

Superhuman Mail is all about speed and a distraction-free experience for busy professionals who live in their inbox. The whole design is built for fast triage, minimal clutter, and deep focus, letting you fly through emails with keyboard shortcuts and a clean interface. If your main pain is email overload and you want to process personal or team emails as efficiently as possible, Superhuman Mail is hands down the better choice.

Front, on the other hand, only really shines if your team's main support happens across multiple channels like social and chat, not just email. But if you're looking for pure email efficiency, Front just doesn't match Superhuman Mail's speed, focus, or workflow for people who need to process a lot of email quickly.

So if your work is email-heavy and you want to get through it fast with no distractions, go with Superhuman Mail. Only pick Front if your whole support workflow is already spread across email, chat, and social, and you truly need everything in one place. Otherwise, Superhuman Mail is the clear winner for email efficiency.

app.name: Superhuman Mail
app.description: Get through your inbox 2x as fast (for teams of all sizes).

children.text: Vimcal gets you through meeting scheduling faster than anything else, while Superhuman Mail focuses on blazing through emails efficiently.
Only use Vimcal if scheduling meetings quickly is your main issue, use Superhuman Mail for handling email and skip it for calendar needs.

If your main goal is scheduling a ton of complex meetings fast, especially if you're an executive assistant wrangling lots of participants across time zones, Vimcal is built for you. It's designed to crush heavy-duty scheduling with features like group polling and natural language event creation, all from a dedicated calendar interface. For people managing high-volume, high-stakes calendars, Vimcal cuts out the back-and-forth and locks down group meetings in a way Superhuman Mail just doesn't try to match.

If you mostly live in your inbox and want to turn emails into events without breaking your flow, Superhuman Mail nails that. Its calendar is tightly integrated with email, so you can create events or check your schedule instantly with a shortcut. The AI assistant and natural language make adding meetings from messages feel effortless, especially for solo professionals or teams who want to keep everything in one streamlined place.

But if you're measuring purely on scheduling efficiency for heavy meeting coordination, Vimcal is the one that actually moves the needle for high-volume schedulers. Superhuman Mail is great for quick, individual scheduling from email, but it's not built to handle the multi-person, multi-time zone puzzles that Vimcal was made for.

So: choose Vimcal if you're the person responsible for getting a dozen busy people in a room at the same time. Go with Superhuman Mail if you want your calendar to disappear into your email workflow, but don't need advanced scheduling muscle.
Superhuman Mail moves through email way faster and is actively supported, while Tatem is minimal but no longer being developed.
Only use Tatem if you absolutely have to, but pick Superhuman Mail for a much smoother and more reliable experience.
Superhuman Mail moves through emails fast and keeps things minimal, while Skiff was shut down after being acquired by Notion.
Only use Superhuman Mail since Skiff is no longer available.
Superhuman Mail is still running and delivers a super fast, clean email experience with deep-work features, keyboard navigation, AI tools, and team collaboration for anyone who lives in their inbox.
Skiff is no longer available after being acquired and shut down.
If you need an email client, Superhuman Mail is the only real option here. Skiff simply isn't usable anymore.

Superhuman Mail feels lightning fast and minimal, letting you and your team clear out email faster than Missive, which focuses more on organizing team discussions and handling messages from different platforms.
Only use Missive if your team absolutely needs to collaborate on emails and messages in one spot, otherwise, Superhuman Mail is the clear pick.
Superhuman Mail gets you through email way faster and stays minimal, while Inbox Zero focuses on labeling, organizing, and bulk unsubscribing.
Only use Inbox Zero if you need open-source tools for labeling and unsubscribing or require Google Workspace or Microsoft support, otherwise go with Superhuman Mail.

If you care about getting through your inbox as fast as possible with minimal distractions, Superhuman Mail was made for exactly that. It's built for busy professionals who live in email and want everything to move instantly, with a clean interface, deep keyboard shortcut support, and a design that cuts out anything that slows you down. You can triage, snooze, search, and even draft responses at top speed, and the whole experience feels like it's made for people who want to power through email without losing focus.

Inbox Zero, on the other hand, is all about using AI to automate labeling, forwarding, and especially unsubscribing from newsletters. If your main headache is dealing with a flood of unwanted emails or you're looking for open-source tools to help with bulk organization, it's a solid pick. But it doesn't come close to matching the sheer speed, workflow focus, or deep-work vibe of Superhuman Mail.

If your priority is true inbox efficiency and feeling in control while you process a lot of emails daily, Superhuman Mail is the clear choice. If you mostly want to clean up and automate your inbox through bulk actions, or you care about open-source, then Inbox Zero is worth a look. For raw speed and a focused experience, nothing beats Superhuman Mail.
Superhuman Mail moves through emails fast and stays minimal, while Hey has limitations for business use and uses an outdated SMPT connection.
Only use Hey if you have no professional needs at all; stick with Superhuman Mail for anything work related or efficient triage.

wrapper for Gmail
with a few extra features designed for hardcore Notion users (e.g. the ability to use Notion AI to reference Notion pages when composing an email), while Superhuman is an email app built for
busy professionals
who want to spend less time in their inbox.

I've been using Superhuman Mail for 6+ years, so when Notion Mail came out, I was curious to give it a shot. I connected my inbox and tried to use it as my daily email app, but within a day I realized it was nowhere near as thought out as Superhuman Mail.

With Superhuman Mail, I can triage my entire inbox incredibly quickly: snooze emails, schedule replies, mute threads, archive messages, and jump between Split Inboxes without touching my mouse. With Notion Mail, I kept breaking flow because I had to pause, reach for my mouse, as the features weren't
all
built to be keyboard first. It looked nice, but it slowed me down. The AI features were no where near Superhuman Mail's and at that point, I knew it couldn't be my main
email client
Views vs Split Inboxes
Notion and Superhuman both offer the ability to split similar types of emails into different "views" (as Notion calls them) or Split Inboxes (as Superhuman Mail calls them).

Essentially, with both tools, you can either use AI to auto-categorize emails or manually set up a filter yourself that helps segment your inbox. This helps you triage through "like" emails at the same time, aiding with context switching and saving time.

Superhuman Mail takes this one step further with its workflows feature. You can set up rules for how you'd like emails to be handled (e.g., use AI to auto-respond to a job application, auto-forward to your hiring manager, and mark as done in your inbox, all without you touching it).
AI Features
They hardly compare in terms of their
AI email assistant
features.
Notion Mail's AI is similar to what you'd get in
Gmail
, while Superhuman Mail actually drafts email responses
in your tone.

YES, in your voice. It learns from your previous messages and responds as you would. AND it drafts emails for you automatically. An email lands in your inbox, and Superhuman Mail writes the reply. All you need to do is review it and send.

Second, Superhuman Mail even figures out if an email you sent needs a follow-up/nudge, and it automatically drafts a follow-up in your voice and leaves it in your inbox for you to hit send (just like an executive assistant would 🤯).

Third, Superhuman Mail uses AI to search your inbox. Forget trying to find the perfect search term to surface an email. With Superhuman Mail, you can ask any question in plain language, and it will give you an AI summary response (e.g., "Where was the conversation left off with XYZ? When is the last time I spoke to XYZ?").
The one advantage Notion Mail has is its integration with
Notion

Pages. You can use Notion AI to reference a Notion Page, and it will draft a response based on the context of the page. That said, you'd have to be a very heavy Notion user for this to offer an advantage.

Superhuman Mail is hands down the better email app.

super

and have at-least a handful of emails that come in on a day-to-day basis? If so, Superhuman is sure to make this experience just a tad bit more enjoyable, bet of yet it integrates directly with Outlook so it'll work out of the box.
Enter

Try a free month of Superhuman

Superhuman simply helps make checking your emails less dreadful and more enjoyable. Every action, including loading, is nearly instantaneous (unlike Gmail). It has a minimal and beautifully designed interface, along with quick shortcut keys to get through your inbox more quickly. We've used Superhuman for 6+ years and definitely think it's worth it compared to using Gmail.
If you're unsure, grab a
Superhuman Mail free trial

Love or hate Gmail, Google has to consider billions of users with every single change that they roll out, this generally slows down their ability to innovate and build innovative features that the masses might not need. That said, when comparing Gmail vs Outlook (Microsoft 365), it is infinitely better, in our eyes. The biggest frustration though is that it's getting quite bloated with unnecessary widgets (e.g. now with the chat and everything there). In-fact, as an avid Gmail user for over a decade, I began finding that I have the thought to send an email, and by the time Gmail loads, I've totally forgotten what I was even about to do.

better

email experience, both on the web and on mobile. The beautiful minimal interface that loads exceptionally quick not only saves us time, but makes the entire email inbox experience much more manageable.

It's wildly fast, minimal (not bloated with features), and helps individuals and teams triage through emails most efficiently. So why are you looking for an alternative?
My best guess is price.

Is Superhuman Worth It?

Features to Consider

Most recently, Superhuman added the ability to even leave private comments for your team within each email thread.
Superhuman Verdict
So my 2 cents? I get you're looking for an alternative. That said, Superhuman Mail now has a

items.name: Supercut
items.description: Video messaging that moves work forward

children.text: Supercut nails async team collaboration and makes recording and sharing way smoother than Tella, especially if you need features like quick timestamped comments.
Only use Tella if you want to put extra effort into tutorials or courses; choose Supercut for anything else, especially team workflows.

Supercut stands out for async team collaboration and efficient screen recording in a work context. It's become the go-to for replacing meetings and handling internal or customer communication, with people preferring to get a Supercut over traditional calls or even other tools like Loom. If you want to streamline team workflows and cut back on meetings, Supercut is the obvious pick.

Tella, on the other hand, shines when you care about editing and video polish. If you're making tutorials or courses where you want to stitch clips together or create something more polished, Tella makes that easier without needing a separate video editor. But it falls short for team collaboration since it's missing features like timestamped comments and doesn't match Supercut for quick async back-and-forth.

If you need the best tool for async team communication and screen recording, go with Supercut. If your focus is on creating and editing educational content or polished videos, Tella is the better fit. For most business screen recording needs, Supercut clearly leads.

app.name: Supercut
app.description: Video messaging that moves work forward

children.text: Supercut nails async team collaboration and feels great to use, while Screen Studio is about making the best looking product videos and GIFs.
Only use Screen Studio if your main goal is to show off interfaces or post polished clips to social media, otherwise pick Supercut.

If you want to make async team communication smoother and actually replace meetings with screen recordings, Supercut is the clear pick. Teams love getting a Supercut instead of a meeting invite, and it's become the standard for internal and external communication at companies that care about async workflows.

But if your main goal is to make your product look incredible on video, especially for demos, social media, or tutorials, Screen Studio stands out. Its automatic zoom, smooth cursor, and built-in editing make your recordings look polished without any pro skills. Screen Studio is the favorite for anyone trying to create eye-catching, professional videos for public sharing or marketing.

Go Supercut if your focus is async collaboration and internal communication. Go Screen Studio if you want the slickest, most impressive product videos for external audiences. Both are great, but each one dominates for its own use case.
Supercut makes async team collaboration easy and actually feels modern, while Loom has become bloated and frustrating to use.
Only use Loom if you have no other choice; Supercut is just better for recording and sharing with your team.
If you're still using Loom, I promise you there is a better path.

We were obsessed with Loom when it first came out, but the Atlassian acquisition destroyed it. It became super buggy and slow. It would crash and we'd need to re-start our filming 2-3 times just to capture it. It got bloated and more cluttered, honestly it was just frustrating and unreliable in our day-to-day, especially when we were trying to move quickly.
That's what pushed us to start looking for alternatives. We tried a handful of tools like
Tella
and Cap, but nothing really simplified our workflow or felt like a true upgrade. Then we came across Supercut, and we immediately absolutely love it (Read our full
Supercut Review
). It felt like what Loom used to be, but modern, faster, way more intentional, and most importantly, reliable.
Key Feature Comparisons
Recorder

Even just opening the recorder is night and day. Loom feels like everything is fighting for your attention. It takes multiple clicks before you ever start recording. With Supercut, it's just... clean. Everything is laid out for you; the UI is minimal, no clutter in sight, and no annoying upsells.

After you've recorded, Supercut keeps that seamless professional feel. With Loom, there's always a ton of pop-ups telling you to sign in, over and over again, but with Supercut, the sharing process is frictionless.
Playback & Transcripts

When someone is watching your video back in Loom, the overwhelm is real. The transcript takes up a huge portion of the screen, the call to record floats over the chapters, and the auto-generated summary takes up what's left of the screen space.

On Supercut, you just get a superior experience. Your video becomes the main focus with chapter, transcripts, comments displayed neatly on the right, and the AI assistant and reaction/comment tucked nicely below your video. Plus, everything loads quickly, and there's no lag in sight.
AI Features

Once you've recorded your video and go to watch it back, the difference becomes really clear. Supercuts AI assistant lets you "ask anything", pulling insights or even turning your video into usable documentation. The first time I realized it could generate full SOPs, I literally said, "Oh my god, this is so good!" out loud.

Looms AI doesn't feel nearly as intuitive, and it's locked behind yet another upgrade. We were constantly being prompted to upgrade tiers without ever really understanding what we were getting. Supercut keeps it simple: if you're on Pro, you have access to everything. That clarity makes it feel so much safer to actually build your workflows around.
Sales

Both Loom and Supercut let you add branding and calls to action, but if you're aiming for a more polished, professional experience overall, we recommend Supercut. It simply feels more refined from start to finish.
Final Thoughts

Supercut as a tool is great, and the video recording abilities got us excited, but it's what it enabled that made us fans. We were able to completely replace a huge chunk of our meetings, reduce back-and-forth with clients, and start documenting processes without thinking twice. It just became how we operate.
If you're trying to decide between the two, it's really a no brainer, go with Supercut (and don't forget to grab this
Supercut discount

items.name: Sunsama
items.description: The digital daily planner that helps you feel calm and stay focused.

children.text: Sunsama delivers a smooth daily planning experience that feels intentional and lets you pull in tasks from other apps, while Trello just gives you a basic kanban board that feels stuck in the past.
Only use Trello if your team relies on Atlassian tools or you need a super simple board; otherwise, Sunsama is the better pick for actually planning your day.

If you want the best experience for intentional, manual daily planning, Sunsama is miles ahead. It's built for people who love the ritual of planning their day, guiding you with thoughtful prompts and a calming interface, and lets you pull in tasks from other tools to schedule them with full control. Everything is manual by design, so you get a focused, pen-and-paper style planning flow, perfect if you want to slow down and be more mindful about your time.

Trello doesn't even try to compete here. It's a classic kanban board project manager, not a daily planner, and while it's great for basic board view and deep Atlassian integrations, it has zero features aimed at daily planning, intention setting, or structured routines. If you're craving a digital daily planner that helps you be deliberate with your schedule, Trello just isn't built for that job.

So if your priority is daily planning with intention and reflection, Sunsama is the clear pick. Trello is only worth considering if all you want is a simple kanban board for tasks, not an actual planning experience.

app.name: Sunsama
app.description: The digital daily planner that helps you feel calm and stay focused.

children.text: Sunsama gives you a smooth daily planning experience with easy integration of tasks from other apps, while Superlist feels buggy and struggles with collaboration even for solo use.
Only use Superlist if you really want playful design and sounds, but skip it for now and go with Sunsama if you want reliable daily planning.

If you want a daily planning tool built around intentional routines and mindful time blocking, Sunsama is in a league of its own. Everything is manual and deliberate, from dragging tasks onto your calendar to reflecting at the end of the day. It's perfect if you like the ritual of planning and want a digital upgrade to pen-and-paper, but it won't automate your schedule or handle project management for you. The integrations let you pull in tasks from other apps, but you still have to choose what actually gets your attention each day.

Superlist goes all in on satisfying design and playful interactions, making checking off tasks genuinely fun. It's strong as a personal task manager and lets you assign to-dos to others, but it's still rough around the edges, especially with collaboration. Bugs get in the way, and even basic teamwork can feel unreliable right now.

If you're after a calm, structured, and mindful daily planning experience, Sunsama is the clear pick. Superlist is great for beautiful, simple lists and playful vibes, but it doesn't match Sunsama's depth or intentionality for daily planning. Go with Sunsama if daily structure matters most. Superlist is only worth a look if you want a fun, lightweight personal list app and can live with some bugs.
Sunsama makes daily planning feel intentional and smooth, while Notion tries to do everything but only really works for team docs.
Only use Notion if your main need is a team knowledge base; stick with Sunsama if you care about daily planning and pulling in tasks from other tools.

If you want intentional, structured daily planning that actually helps you focus on what matters each day, Sunsama is the clear pick. Its whole approach is built around guiding you through a mindful routine, asking what you want to get done, helping you schedule it, and then ending with a daily reflection. Everything is manual and deliberate, which is perfect if you like the pen-and-paper ritual but want it digital. You get a calming design, real time blocking, and a process that keeps you honest about your time.

Notion, on the other hand, is mainly a knowledge base that can technically be turned into a daily planner, but only if you're ready to spend a lot of time setting up and maintaining your own system from scratch. Most people end up overwhelmed or distracted by tinkering, and the daily planning just doesn't stick unless you force it. Even then, adoption breaks down because the structure rarely makes sense to anyone but the person who set it up.

If daily planning is your goal, Sunsama nails it out of the box and actually makes it a habit. Notion just isn't built for this and quickly turns into a mess if you try. Go with Sunsama if you want daily planning that works.

Sunsama gives you a thoughtfully designed daily planning experience and lets you pull in tasks from other apps, while Fantastical is mainly for basic personal or family calendars and is not recommended even for that.
Only use Fantastical if you want a simple family calendar, but choose Sunsama for a more intentional daily planning routine.

Sunsama is built for people who want a structured, intentional daily planning ritual. Its whole design revolves around helping you map out your day with purpose, using features like daily planning prompts, manual time blocking, and end-of-day reflection. You get a clear, calming interface that lets you pull in tasks from other tools but requires you to choose what matters and when to do it. This is perfect for students, solo operators, or professionals who want to slow down and be deliberate with their time, not just race through a packed schedule.

Fantastical, on the other hand, isn't recommended for professional use at all. The app feels dated and lacks the modern planning features or integrations that make daily planning effective. Even for personal use, it doesn't stand out, and there are better options suggested for both personal and professional users.

If you care about daily planning and want a tool that guides you through a mindful routine, Sunsama is the obvious pick. Fantastical doesn't come close on this front and isn't recommended for anyone serious about planning their day with intention.
Sunsama nails daily planning with a smooth design and keeps the act of planning intentional, while Amie now focuses on being an AI notetaker and has sidelined its calendar and to-do features.
Only use Amie if you want an AI notetaker without a meeting bot; stick with Sunsama for daily planning.

Sunsama is built entirely around daily planning, making it the clear pick if you want a tool that guides you through intentional time blocking and structured routines. It's all about helping you sit down, reflect, and design your day with purpose, especially if you're someone who already loves the pen-and-paper approach and wants a digital version that doesn't skip the mindful parts.

Amie, on the other hand, has shifted away from focusing on daily planning. Its calendar and to-do features are now sidelined, with the main focus being meeting recording and AI note-taking. If your main goal is daily planning, Amie just isn't prioritizing that anymore.
If what matters most to you is a dedicated, thoughtful daily planning workflow, Sunsama is the right call. Amie simply doesn't compete here now that its priorities have changed.
Sunsama delivers a smooth daily planning experience and makes scheduling tasks from other apps easy, while Monday tries to do too much and ends up feeling unfocused.
Only use Monday if you need something extremely broad, but Sunsama is the clear pick if you want a focused, intentional daily planner.

If you want something that's effortless to get started with and focused purely on daily planning, Sunsama is miles ahead. It's clean, intuitive, and makes the process of mapping out your day feel natural, especially if you like the ritual of intentional planning. You just open it up, drag your tasks onto your calendar, and you're good to go, no setup headaches or consultant needed.

Monday, on the other hand, is a beast to set up. It tries to do everything, but that means actually getting it working for your team is a project in itself. For most people (especially small teams or anyone who isn't ready to spend serious time mapping processes and documenting workflows), it just sits unused after the initial excitement because it's too much work to implement.

So if your goal is to get a daily planning tool up and running right away, Sunsama is the obvious pick. It's designed for people who want a simple, manual, and thoughtful workflow. Monday only makes sense if you're ready to invest a lot of time (or pay someone) to set it up as a full project management system, and even then, it's not about daily planning. For fast adoption and ease of use in daily planning, Sunsama wins, no contest.
Linear nails project management for engineers and is built around engineering workflows, while Sunsama focuses on making daily planning feel intentional and easy.
Only use Sunsama if you care most about the process of planning your day and want to pull in tasks from other apps; otherwise use Linear.

If you care about daily planning and intentional time blocking, Sunsama is the clear pick. It's built for people who want a manual, ritual-driven approach to structuring their day, giving you a calm interface to drag tasks onto your calendar, reflect, and review at the end of each day. It doesn't automate your schedule or try to handle project management, but it nails the daily planner vibe for those who enjoy setting aside time each morning to map things out.

Linear isn't even playing in this space. It's not a daily planner and doesn't provide those reflective or manual scheduling routines. It's focused on engineering and product workflows, tying together bugs, features, and sprints. If you're an engineer managing team projects, Linear is fantastic, but it won't help you with daily personal planning or intentional time blocking.

If your goal is to thoughtfully plan your day and stay mindful about your time, Sunsama is the tool to grab. If you want project management for engineering teams, go with Linear, but for daily planning, it's Sunsama all the way.

Asana demands time and resources for setup and ongoing manual upkeep, which makes it feel heavy unless you have a large team and a dedicated project manager, while Sunsama offers an effortless daily planning experience focused on intentionality.
Only use Asana if you need complex project management and have the resources for it; otherwise, stick with Sunsama for a smoother, simpler day-to-day workflow.

If you want a tool for daily planning that feels like a digital pen-and-paper experience, Sunsama nails it. It's all about giving you control, making you pause, plan, and reflect each day. The manual approach is intentional, not a shortcut, and it's perfect if you actually enjoy the daily ritual and want to be mindful with your time.

Asana, on the other hand, is built for managing complex projects with lots of moving parts, teams, and reporting needs. Daily planning is not its focus. It's heavy, setup-intensive, and best for organizations that need robust project management, not for people looking to simply organize and plan their day with intention.

If your priority is a beautifully designed, mindful daily planning routine, Sunsama is the clear pick. If you're after daily planning, Asana will just slow you down and get in the way. Go with Sunsama for daily planning, Asana isn't built for it.
Sunsama makes planning your day feel intentional and smooth, while Todoist just covers basic task management without adding much else.
Only use Todoist if you want something simple for personal tasks, but choose Sunsama if you care about daily planning and integrations.

If you care about daily planning and time blocking, Sunsama is in a different league from Todoist. Sunsama is built entirely around the ritual of intentional daily planning, guiding you through a process each morning and evening with a clear focus on mindfulness and healthy work habits. You get a full calendar view side by side with your tasks, drag-and-drop scheduling, and thoughtful routines that force you to be realistic about your time. The experience is designed for people who want to sit down, reflect, and make conscious decisions about how they spend each day.

Todoist, on the other hand, treats time blocking as an afterthought. Its calendar view and time blocking features are minimal and not the main event. If you just want to quickly make lists and check off tasks, Todoist delivers, but if you're looking for a real daily planning workflow, it falls short. Many Todoist users end up integrating it with a tool like Sunsama to get the full planning experience.
So if daily planning and structured time blocking are what matter to you, Sunsama is the clear choice. Todoist simply doesn't compete on this front.
Sunsama delivers a smooth daily planning experience and feels intentional to use, while ClickUp tries to do too much and ends up slow and clunky.
Only use ClickUp if you really need its everything-in-one approach; otherwise, Sunsama is the better pick for planning your day.

Sunsama absolutely nails daily planning and time blocking with a super clean, calming design and a workflow built for people who want to be intentional about their day. Everything is manual and focused on mindful routines, so if you love sitting down every morning to plan your tasks and reflect at the end of the day, Sunsama makes that experience smooth and rewarding. It's the digital equivalent of a pen-and-paper planner, perfect for solo operators, students, or anyone who wants control and clarity rather than automation or speed.

ClickUp, on the other hand, tries to bundle everything under the sun, tasks, docs, chat, whiteboards, time tracking, but the result is cluttered, overwhelming, and slow. If you just want to manage your day, ClickUp will bury you in options and irrelevant features, making it hard to find what you need and slowing you down with confusing menus and laggy load times. The learning curve is steep, and simple daily planning gets lost in the noise unless you're already deep into the ClickUp ecosystem.

If what matters most is a frictionless, focused daily planning experience, Sunsama is the clear pick. ClickUp only makes sense if you're after an all-in-one project management hub and are willing to deal with complexity and clutter to get it. For daily planning, Sunsama is in another league.
So the truth is, Sunsama
is

one of the best daily planners on the market. It has a great user experience and a beautiful interface, and they have really honed in on their target market: people who love the ritual of daily planning. The people who love Sunsama most are those who come from using pen & paper for planning their tasks, and they enjoy spending 10-15 minutes mindfully planning their day 🧘‍♀️.
But given you're on this page, our guess is this doesn't sound like you, so let's give you some better alternatives.
The first tool to consider (
and what we use, as we don't have time for daily planning rituals when running the business
), is
Motion

Similar to Sunsama, Motion allows you to add in tasks but instead of you needing to manually plan your day, you just tell Motion the priority, deadline, start date, and it will automatically schedule the task into your day. What is this experience like? Well, you wake up, look at your calendar, and you see what you need to work on for that day. It's the closest thing to having and assistant. For us as a team, it more than pays for itself in the time it saves us.
Another popular Sunsama alternative for professionals is
Akiflow

Akiflow is what we'd call a task consolidation tool. Basically, it's a task app, for all the tasks you have across every single software that you use. Have an email to answer? Task in your project management software? Message from Slack? Well, integrate all those apps to Akiflow so you have one master to-do list. Similar to Sunsama, you still need to manually time block tasks on your calendar, but you can also just keep them in a list format and triage through them that way. It's similar to Sunsama but with less of the "ritual planning" and "intentional planning".

Then you have your more barebones Sunsama alternatives like Morgen Calendar, Amie Calendar, Routine, and Todoist. All of these apps have a free tier, which might sound appealing, but you will be asked to upgrade at a point to unlock certain useful features.
Morgen & Amie are more so calendars first (rather than daily planners first).

They allow you to add basic tasks or reminders on your calendar in time blocks, but they are both way more barebones than Sunsama. If you're looking for a more simple version of Sunsama, either apps are worth trying
(heads up,
Amie
is iOS only at the moment,
Morgen
is Android + iOS).
Routine
is a basic daily planner

where you can add tasks and drag them onto your calendar, although it's still more basic than Sunsama. They are in building mode so they currently have a free tier, but unsure how long that will last.
Finally,
Todoist
is a Sunsama alternative if you just want an app to throw tasks in.

While Todoist has a calendar view available on their Pro plan, it's also quite barebones compared to Sunsama. Todoist was always known for their simple tasks lists, and they recently introduced a calendar view due to popular demand from their customers. That said, it's a secondary feature rather than the main way folks use Todoist.

items.name: Littlebird
items.description: The only full-context AI

children.text: Littlebird pulls in context from your meetings, calendar, email, and on-screen activity, while ChatGPT does not mention any of that.
Only use ChatGPT if you want something simple and proven we use daily; skip Littlebird unless you need the extra context from your work.

Littlebird stands out by actively pulling in context from your meetings, calendar, email, and even what's happening on your screen. If you want an AI assistant that actually knows what you're doing throughout your day and can help recall or act on that, Littlebird is the only one here that delivers that level of context awareness. The catch is you need to be comfortable with a newer startup having access to all that personal and work data.

ChatGPT, on the other hand, doesn't track or integrate with your day-to-day activities. It's a general-purpose AI chatbot that anyone can use for answering questions, writing code, or brainstorming ideas, but it won't know what's on your calendar or what you just discussed in a meeting. It's the go-to if you want something powerful and easy to use right away, no setup needed, and you don't need it to be aware of your workflow.

If what you care about is an AI assistant that's actually aware of your daily work and can use that context to help you, Littlebird is the clear pick. But if you just want a smart chatbot without giving up access to your digital life, ChatGPT is the safer and simpler choice.

app.name: Littlebird
app.description: The only full-context AI

children.text: Claude nails writing, coding, and research as a smart partner, but unlike Littlebird, it does not pull context from your meetings, calendar, or on-screen activity.
Only use Littlebird if you need an assistant that actually tracks what you're working on across meetings and apps; otherwise Claude is the clear pick.

Claude stands out for handling deeper, more complete writing and reasoning tasks, especially if you already have a detailed writing brief or skill set to import. It automatically references your instructions in every chat, so you don't have to repeat yourself or manage context manually. This makes it a better fit if you want an AI partner that actually holds onto your input and gives you real, thoughtful answers instead of endless back-and-forth.

Littlebird's main edge is its ability to pull real-time context from your meetings, calendar, email, and what's on your screen. If you want an assistant that's always aware of what you're working on and can recall details across your day, Littlebird's context awareness is hard to match. But you have to be comfortable giving it broad access to your on-screen activity, and that's a dealbreaker for some.

For pure writing, research, or coding where you care about context persistence and thoughtful responses, Claude is the clear pick. If your priority is having an assistant that knows what you're doing across all your apps and meetings, and you're fine with the privacy tradeoff, Littlebird is the one to grab.
Littlebird pulls in context from your meetings, calendar, email, and even what's on your screen, while Gong sticks to analyzing sales calls, emails, and meetings just for enterprise sales teams.
Only use Gong if you run a large sales team with complex deals; otherwise, stick with Littlebird for broader and more useful context.

Littlebird stands out by pulling context not just from your meetings, but also your calendar, email, and everything happening on your screen. That means its notes and recall are way more comprehensive, especially if you want an AI note taker that understands the full scope of your workday, not just what happens in meetings.

Gong is laser-focused on sales teams and uses AI to analyze calls, emails, and meetings to surface deal risks and sales insights. It's designed for enterprise sales management, not general note taking or broad day-to-day context.

If you want an AI note taker that's actually useful beyond just call transcripts and can help you remember and act on anything you did during the day, Littlebird is the clear pick. Only go with Gong if your priority is sales process analytics for a big sales team, since its note taking is really just a starting point for deal analysis, not the main event. For everyone else, especially if you want context-rich notes, Littlebird is the better choice.
Littlebird grabs context from your meetings, calendar, email, and what you have on screen, while tl;dv focuses on live team note taking and sharing during calls.
Only use tl;dv if you need everyone to see and edit call notes as they happen, otherwise pick Littlebird for broader context.

If you want meeting notes that everyone can see and edit live, tl;dv stands out. The floating pop-up lets your team take notes together during the call, instantly timestamps everything, and pushes the results to everyone or just your team, with Slack integration for quick sharing. This is built for people who need real-time, collaborative note taking in meetings.

Littlebird takes a different approach. It's all about pulling context from your whole workday, not just meetings, by recording your calls and tracking on-screen activity. If you're comfortable with that level of access, you'll get meeting notes that are richer and more aware of what you've been doing across all your apps. But it's less about live, shared note taking and more about creating a personal record that ties together everything you do.

So if you care most about fast, collaborative meeting notes everyone can use right away, tl;dv is the clear pick. If you want notes that are deeply connected to all your activity for personal recall and don't need real-time collaboration, Littlebird's context-driven notes might be better for you.
Littlebird actually knows what you're doing by pulling context from your meetings, calendar, email, and on-screen activity, while Cluely just feels like an outdated approach to AI note taking.

Littlebird stands out because it doesn't just take notes in meetings, it actually pulls context from everything you do on your computer. That means when you need to recall a detail or get help, it already knows what you've been working on all day, not just what was said on a call. If you're comfortable with it having that level of access, you get way more value and relevance from its suggestions and recall.

Cluely tries to be helpful during calls by recommending questions based on the conversation, but it's really just surfacing prompts and info in the moment. It listens and suggests things as you talk, but it doesn't have the deeper context of your overall workflow or workday. Its approach is more about being proactive in the call itself, but it's not fundamentally smarter or more integrated than just using a chat app on the side.

So you're looking for an expense management solution and you're deciding between
BILL Spend & Expense
(formerly Divvy) and
Ramp
. Well, we've used both and we're here to to tell you, they are
both great options
but there is a clear way to decide which one is right for you 👀
Ramp vs BILL Similarities

As a baseline, both Ramp and BILL offer virtual and physical corporate cards, that offer rewards, spend management tools, and have no annual fees. They both have card controls (budgets), spend tracking, and will allow you to spin up new employee cards within seconds (or cancel them just as quickly 😄). When integrated with Quickbooks Online, they will also both allow you to automatically reconcile your books.
Ramp vs BILL Differences
Bank Minimums

The size of your business makes a difference in terms of which expense management platform you choose. While both Ramp and BILL are free to use, they have different minimum bank bank balances in order to get approved for an account.
BILL Spend & Expense Bank Minimum: $0
Ramp Bank Minimum: $25,000
Even though Divvy was acquired by Bill, they still maintain $0 bank minimums to get approved for an account.
So an easy decision can be made—do you currently have $50,000 in your bank account? If not, stop your research and opt for
BILL Spend & Expense
Business Size

If it's not clear from the bank minimum requirements, Ramp is targeting slightly larger companies than BILL. If you're under 10-20 employees, BILL is likely more suited for you. If you're are larger team, or a start-up who just raised money looking to scale, Ramp is likely better for you.
Insights

This is where Ramp really shines. Ramp can send you data on duplicate spending and cost saving opportunities with different vendors. It can alert you if you have two people paying for the same service, for example. If you're a start-up, scaling company, or have many employees, Ramp would be best for you.
Credit Card Rewards

Ramp provides a straightforward 1.5% cash back on all purchases, while BILL offers up to 7x points on specific categories like restaurants and hotels. With BILL, the rewards are better if you pay your balance off weekly (vs semi-monthly or monthly), so we recommend doing that to optimize your cash back.
Quick story—about two years ago we introduced a small
construction company
(<10 person team) to BILL. The owner decided to use the cash back at the end of the year as a Christmas Bonus for his employees. This was "found money" that he wouldn't have otherwise had.
This construction company uses it's cash back for employee bonuses each year.
What would we choose?

Years ago, one of our clients asked if he could pay an invoice through Divvy (now BILL Spend & Expense). Upon looking into it, we realized just how awesome the platform was and decided we needed to use it for ourselves. There was no bank minimum, it was totally free, and we got rewards on our spending,
AKA It was a no-brainer

Upon researching other similar solutions on the market, we came across Ramp, and with the $25K bank minimum ($75K bank minimum at the time, which they seem to be reducing), it was something that our business just didn't have at the time.
So the decision was made many years ago
and we're still using BILL Spend & Expense today.
That said, as our business grew and we had $50–100K in our bank account, we decided to apply for a Ramp account, knowing we'd probably love it. And the truth is, we did 😬
They have a beautiful UI and friendly UX, and offer cool insights into your spending. But at this point, the switching costs for us would be ultimately too high to even consider swapping to Ramp.
So we'll leave you with this, if you can meet the minimum bank requirements, give Ramp a shot (it's probably what we'd do). If you can't, don't fret—BILL Spend & Expense is an awesome choice!
