That means total control, tighter security (catching fraud is actually possible), and seamless integration with Quickbooks for automatic reconciliation.

Expensify still leans on old-school receipt forwarding and scraping. Even though they now offer virtual cards, they're playing catch up and don't match the smooth upfront categorization or fine-grained card control you get with BILL Spend & Expense. If you just want to forward receipts and don't care about proactive spend management, Expensify is fine. But if you want to actually manage and control spending as it happens, BILL Spend & Expense is the clear pick.
BILL Spend & Expense is free, easy to access, and does not require bank minimums, while Airwallex focuses on letting you handle and spend multiple currencies for international business.
Only use Airwallex if you need to manage and spend money across different countries; stick with BILL Spend & Expense for simple, no-fee expense management.

If you're running a small or medium business in the US and want the easiest, most accessible expense management with full control, BILL Spend & Expense is the obvious pick. You get unlimited virtual cards, instant spend limits, and super simple employee card management, all with no fees or minimums. The eligibility bar is low, so even new businesses can get started. It nails the basics and makes expense tracking, budgeting, and even fraud prevention totally painless.

Airwallex only stands out if your business deals with multiple currencies or operates globally. Its real advantage is letting you hold, spend, and pay vendors in different currencies without forced conversions or extra FX fees. The expense management tools are solid, but they're designed for companies juggling international transactions, not for straightforward US-only operations.

So, if you just want the best expense management for a US-based business, go with BILL Spend & Expense. If your top priority is managing spending and expenses across borders and currencies, that's where Airwallex makes sense. For almost everyone else, BILL Spend & Expense is the clear winner here.

items.name: Buffer
items.description: All-you-need social media platform for small businesses.

children.text: We were so much more
impressed with Buffer

than Hootsuite. Buffer has a more modern interface and offers a better user experience. Not only that, you can use Buffer on the free tier and it's still 10x better than the $99/month version of Hootsuite 😅

What you won't get with Buffer is as in-depth analytics and reporting (e.g. if you are an enterprise company have managers managing managers and you need deep analytics and reports then Hootsuite will likely be a better fit). That said, for small or medium sized businesses,
Buffer is the clear winner.

app.name: Buffer
app.description: All-you-need social media platform for small businesses.

children.text: We've used Buffer for posting short form videos to our brand account on X and LinkedIn, but that was because we also were posting the same video content as Reels on Instagram. Buffer allowed us to post to all three platforms at once, which saved a ton of time. So Buffer is a better choice if you're wanting to post social content to various social media channels (Instagram, TikTok, etc).

But if you're mainly wanting to grow your X or LinkedIn following and don't care about the other social platforms, then Typefully is a better choice as you'll get features designed to specifically improve your posting experience on those platforms.

Buffer covers more platforms and nails Instagram scheduling, with a smoother interface and higher reliability across Instagram, LinkedIn, and X. If you want one tool to handle posting everywhere and especially care about Instagram, Buffer is the clear pick.

Typefully goes deep on X and LinkedIn, offering advanced features like Auto-Retweet and detailed analytics. If growing your X or LinkedIn following is your main goal and you don't care about Instagram or other platforms, Typefully is the stronger choice.

But for most people looking to schedule posts across multiple social media accounts, Buffer is simply more versatile and dependable. Only pick Typefully if your whole focus is X or LinkedIn. Otherwise, Buffer wins.
We initially tried to use Metricool for social media posting but came out super disappointed on the other end. Then we found Buffer and the experience was
so much more seamless.
Buffer tends to have a more modern interface and through our testing, we found it to be more reliable in terms of actually getting our posts published. Buffer is the clear winner between the two.

items.name: Dia Browser
items.description: Dia is an AI work browser that turns your tabs into a teammate for writing, research, and decisions.

children.text: Claude handles work, coding, and writing directly and stands out as a strong thinking or research partner, while Dia Browser adds AI chat into an Arc-like browser with standout sidebar and tab management.
Stick with Claude unless you specifically want Arc Browser features with AI chat at the center, then use Dia Browser.

Claude pulls ahead if you actually care about how well the chatbot remembers and uses your context. It grabs your writing briefs or skills automatically every time, so you don't have to remind it or set anything up for each new chat. That adds up fast if you're bouncing between projects or prompts throughout the day. The context window is longer, and once you load in your instructions, Claude starts asking sharper questions and actually tailors its responses, not just going through the motions.

Dia Browser's chat is slick and looks great, but it can't use your actual ChatGPT history, so you're missing out on deeper context if you've built up a lot of prompts or reference material elsewhere. Its "Skills" are nice for running pre-built prompts with page context, but it doesn't match Claude's habit of weaving in your personal briefs automatically.

If you want a chatbot that really "gets" your instructions and keeps your context front and center, Claude is the clear pick. Dia Browser is more about a beautiful browser experience with AI on the side, but for context awareness in chat, it just doesn't keep up.

app.name: Dia Browser
app.description: Dia is an AI work browser that turns your tabs into a teammate for writing, research, and decisions.

children.text: Workona lets you keep using Chrome or Safari while organizing tabs into workspaces, while Dia Browser requires switching browsers but brings over Arc's sidebar, tab management, and profiles with an AI chatbot built in.
Only use Workona if you refuse to leave your current browser; choose Dia Browser if you want Arc's features plus AI and are open to switching.

If you care most about pure tab management, Dia Browser is a clear step up, its tab management is described as exceptional, refined, and has set the standard that others (including Chrome) have tried to copy. Dia builds on what made Arc Browser's tab features great, and the review calls them "more refined than ever." If you're willing to switch browsers, Dia gives you the smoothest experience for organizing, switching, and working with lots of tabs.

Workona is strong if you refuse to leave Chrome or Safari. It massively upgrades your existing browser's tab management with workspaces and lets you avoid switching browsers entirely. But even its own review admits that Dia handles actual tab management better. Workona is for people set on their current browser who just want a smarter way to juggle tabs, not the absolute best system out there.

If you want the best tab management and are open to using a new browser, pick Dia Browser. If you're stuck on Chrome or Safari and refuse to change, Workona does a good job, but it's not the top choice for this.
Dia Browser brings over the standout sidebar, strong tab management, and profiles from Arc, plus adds an AI chatbot, while Toby just gives you a nicer new tab page and only light tab control.
Only use Toby if you want the simplest setup possible; choose Dia Browser for anything more demanding.

Dia Browser does real tab management with an exceptional sidebar and features built for actually handling lots of tabs and complex workflows. Toby just gives you a nicer new tab page, but doesn't dig into real tab chaos or offer any deep controls. If you want something that actually helps you stay organized with tons of tabs, Dia is the clear pick.

Toby only makes things prettier without solving the core problem for heavy users. Dia brings over the strong tab management from Arc and pushes it further with AI features. Unless you're after the most lightweight, surface-level fix, Dia is the only one here that actually tackles the mess.
Dia Browser delivers Arc's standout sidebar and tab management plus integrated AI, while Zen Browser copies some features but runs into extension issues and site bugs due to its Firefox base.
Stick with Dia Browser unless you specifically want to avoid Chromium, since Zen Browser trades away compatibility and stability.

Dia Browser is way more efficient and reliable than Zen Browser. Its performance stands out among AI browsers, with a refined sidebar and tab management carried over from Arc, and it delivers a smooth, modern AI chat experience that just works. Zen Browser tries to mimic this but falls short because it's built on Firefox, which means most Chrome or Safari extensions won't work, and you'll probably run into more bugs and weaker developer tools.

If you care about efficiency and a polished experience, Dia is the clear pick. Zen only makes sense if you're deeply committed to Firefox and willing to sacrifice extension support and deal with potential site issues. For everyone else, Dia is the better choice by a mile.

Dia Browser gives you all the standout features from Arc with a built in modern AI chatbot, while Vivaldi only makes sense if you absolutely need to move every button because you dislike standard layouts.
Stick with Dia Browser unless you are obsessed with customizing every detail, in which case Vivaldi is the only reason to switch.

Dia Browser feels modern and fast, with a sleek sidebar and AI chat experience at its core, while Vivaldi still looks and feels like it did in 2015. If you care about having a performant, thoughtfully designed browser with current features, Dia is way ahead.

Vivaldi's only real edge is extreme customization of every button and theme, but that comes at the cost of being stuck with an outdated, uninspiring interface. Unless you absolutely need to rearrange every last UI element and don't mind a dated look, there's no reason to pick Vivaldi over Dia.

For anyone who wants a browser that feels current, smooth, and well-designed, Dia is the obvious choice. Vivaldi is only worth it if obsessive customization is your top priority and you don't care about modern design or features.
Dia Browser gives you all the best features from Arc like the sidebar and tab management, plus built in AI chat, while SigmaOS mostly copies Arc features and charges for them.
Only use SigmaOS if you specifically want what YC is backing, otherwise pick Dia Browser for the full feature set without extra cost.

Dia Browser is just flat-out more efficient than SigmaOS. It carries over the best productivity features from Arc Browser, like a refined sidebar and advanced tab management, and layers in a fast, integrated AI chat experience that actually works well. You get all of this for free unless you really need unlimited AI, and even then, most people won't hit the free tier limits.

SigmaOS tries some new layout ideas and leans into productivity, but a lot of its features are basically Arc copies that you have to pay for. It also just doesn't have the resources or polish of Dia, and there's no sign it can keep up on efficiency or speed.

If efficiency is what matters, Dia Browser is the clear pick. It's faster, more thoughtfully designed, and gives you more at no cost, while SigmaOS feels like a less capable, paywalled version of what Dia already nails.
Dia Browser gives you all the standout Arc Browser features plus a built in AI chatbot, while Sidekick feels outdated and clunky by comparison.
Only use Sidekick if you have a specific need for it, since Dia Browser is a clear upgrade in every way mentioned.

Dia Browser is miles ahead when it comes to modern browser features and design. It carries over everything that made Arc stand out, like its refined sidebar and top-tier tab management, and layers in an AI chat experience that actually works well. The team behind it is focused on pushing browser design forward, with resources to keep innovating.

Sidekick, on the other hand, feels dated. Its so-called productivity features are now basic and found everywhere, and the overall experience doesn't hold up against newer browsers like Dia. The design is behind, and it's no longer even actively developed since the team got pulled into building something else.
If you want a browser that feels current and actually helps you work better, Dia is the clear pick. Sidekick just doesn't keep up.
Dia Browser brings Arc's standout sidebar, tab management, and profiles plus an AI chatbot, while Shift just feels like a Chrome add-on.
Only use Shift if you need its account switching, but Dia Browser is the clear pick for a modern, feature-rich experience.

Dia Browser is in a completely different league when it comes to productivity and overall browser experience. Its sidebar, tab management, and profiles are not just bolted-on features but core strengths that made Arc popular, and now they're even more refined in Dia. The AI chat is seamlessly integrated, and the "Skills" feature lets you interact with page content in ways Shift can't touch.

Shift, on the other hand, basically feels like a thin wrapper on top of Chrome with some account switching and sidebar pinning. It hasn't kept pace with new browsers and feels dated, offering little that you couldn't already get with Chrome plus a few extensions like Workona or Toby.
If you want a browser that actually improves how you work and isn't just a slightly tweaked Chrome, Dia Browser is the clear choice. Shift just doesn't bring enough to the table anymore.

Dia Browser brings the standout features of Arc with a modern AI chatbot at its core, while Safari sticks to a familiar but dull experience and misses the advanced features and enjoyable UX Dia offers.
Only use Safari if you care most about stability and syncing across Apple devices and dislike new features; otherwise, Dia Browser is the clear pick.

Dia Browser blows Safari out of the water when it comes to advanced features, enjoyable user experience, and overall browser innovation. Dia brings over everything that made Arc's sidebar, tab management, and profiles so good, then layers in a modern AI chat assistant that actually feels integrated and useful. The whole experience is more refined, engaging, and genuinely makes daily browsing feel fresh and productive, especially if you like the idea of having pre-built prompts interact with your web pages.

Safari, on the other hand, is all about giving you the same safe, stable, and frankly boring experience it always has. It's reliable and syncs well across Apple devices, but there's nothing here that stands out as exciting or even particularly efficient. The UI is bland by design, and outside of battery life and Apple ecosystem perks, it just can't compete with the thoughtful design and feature set Dia brings.

If you want a browser that actually makes you enjoy using the web and gives you powerful tools right in the sidebar, Dia is the clear choice. Safari only makes sense if you're allergic to change and care about battery life above literally everything else. Everyone else should go with Dia.

Dia Browser brings over Arc's standout sidebar, tab management, and profiles, but adds a modern AI chatbot at its core, while Opera mainly copies Arc's onboarding and features but feels unchanged aside from flashy animations.
Only use Opera if you have a specific attachment, otherwise Dia Browser is the clear pick for a smarter, more modern experience.

Dia Browser absolutely outclasses Opera on core browser design and feature quality. Dia brings over the best parts of Arc's sidebar, tab management, and profiles, all refined and enhanced with a well-integrated AI chat experience. Every feature feels intentional and modern, not just bolted on.

Opera, on the other hand, is mostly playing catch-up. Its new features like Workspaces and tab groups are direct copies from Arc, but they miss critical details, like true environment separation. The flashy animations don't make up for the fact that it's still the same old Opera underneath, just with a new coat of paint.

If you care about thoughtful design, performance, and genuinely useful features, Dia is the clear pick. Opera only makes sense if you're already locked in and just want something familiar with a few trendy upgrades, but it's not competing at the same level. Go with Dia.

items.name: Notion Calendar
items.description: A beautiful and simple calendar for individuals (formerly Cron).

children.text: Notion Calendar gives you a nice looking free calendar for iOS and feels cleaner for basic scheduling than Akiflow, but don't bother trying to manage tasks in it outside of kludgy Notion database items.
Only use Akiflow if you really need to pull tasks from lots of places, but expect overwhelm fast and skip it for simple calendar needs.

If you just want a fast, beautiful calendar that's way nicer to use than Google Calendar, Notion Calendar easily takes it. Its design feels polished, navigation is instant, and keyboard shortcuts make scheduling lightning quick. It's perfect if all you need is a calendar that looks and feels great, without any fuss or overload.

Akiflow is for people who are drowning in tasks from a million apps and desperately need one place to see everything. It excels at pulling in tasks from tools like Slack, Asana, and email, so if your chaos comes from scattered todos and you want to drag them onto your calendar, Akiflow is built for that. But be warned: dump everything in, and you'll quickly feel overwhelmed since it won't help you prioritize or automatically clean things up. It's manual, and falling behind gets messy fast.

So if you want a calendar that just works and never gets in your way, go with Notion Calendar. If your main problem is wrangling tasks from all over the place and you're willing to put in the work to triage them yourself, Akiflow is the tool for that, but only if you keep things focused. For pure calendar use, Notion Calendar is the clear pick. For centralized task management (with the risk of overload), Akiflow stands out.

app.name: Notion Calendar
app.description: A beautiful and simple calendar for individuals (formerly Cron).

children.text: Notion Calendar gives you a clean calendar for iOS and does better than Google Calendar if you just want a simple free calendar, while Routine tries to do too much with knowledge and project management and ends up confusing instead of helping you plan your day.
Only use Routine if you want an all-in-one suite and don't mind a cluttered experience, but pick Notion Calendar if you just need a straightforward calendar.

Notion Calendar (Cron) absolutely nails user experience and interface design, making every interaction feel smooth and intentional. It's fast, minimal, and the keyboard shortcuts actually make using it a pleasure, not a chore. Even little things like multi-day views and smart navigation just work without friction.

Routine, on the other hand, is a cluttered mess by comparison. The interface is clunky, confusing, and basic actions require trial and error. You're just as likely to get lost in database-level settings or accidentally delete something core as you are to actually plan your day. Instead of feeling streamlined, Routine piles on complexity without delivering real power or clarity.

If you're after a calendar that feels good to use and makes daily planning straightforward, Notion Calendar (Cron) is the clear pick. Routine's ambition just gets in the way, while Notion Calendar (Cron) keeps it clean and genuinely enjoyable. Don't waste time fighting a confusing interface when you could be done and out.
Morgen Calendar gives you a faster, better interface and lets you add basic tasks, which Notion Calendar (Cron) does not handle well.
Only use Notion Calendar (Cron) if you just want a free calendar for iOS and do not care about managing tasks or using Outlook.

Notion Calendar (Cron) stands out for its design and speed. The interface is polished down to the smallest detail and every interaction feels fast and deliberate. Keyboard shortcuts, smooth multi-timezone support, and flexible multi-day views make it lightning quick to use, especially if you care about small UX touches. Even editing shared events is better handled, thanks to clever workarounds that most competitors skip.

Morgen Calendar is also a step up from Google Calendar or Outlook, especially if you want a prettier interface with basic task support. It's easy to pick up and works as expected, but the daily planning features are barebones. If you're just dabbling with light planning and don't have a lot to keep track of, Morgen will do the trick, but anything more and you'll hit its limits fast.

If what matters most to you is a beautiful, fast, thoughtfully designed calendar experience, Notion Calendar (Cron) is the clear pick. Morgen is fine for light personal use, but Notion Calendar (Cron) just feels better to use and handles the details that make daily scheduling less of a chore. If you want a calendar that's genuinely pleasant to use every day, go with Notion Calendar (Cron).
Amie focuses on AI note taking without a meeting bot joining your calls, while Notion Calendar (Cron) is mainly for visually appealing calendar use on iOS.
Only use Notion Calendar (Cron) if you just want a free calendar for iOS, but pick Amie if you need an AI note taker and don't care about task management.

If you want the best calendar experience, Notion Calendar (Cron) is the clear pick. The app stands out for its fast, minimal, and thoughtfully designed interface, with smooth interactions and details that actually make using your calendar enjoyable. Features like flexible multi-day views, smart keyboard shortcuts, and clever workarounds for shared events make it feel polished and efficient. If all you need is a great calendar, especially one that feels better than Google Calendar, this is the one to use.

Amie, on the other hand, has shifted focus away from its calendar and to-do features, putting its energy into being an AI notetaker and meeting recorder. The calendar is no longer a priority for them, so you won't get the same level of attention or polish for scheduling and event management.
If your priority is a calendar that feels well-designed and works smoothly day to day, go with Notion Calendar (Cron). Amie just isn't focused on that anymore.
Todoist keeps personal tasks organized and simple, while Notion Calendar (Cron) only makes sense if you want a free calendar that's nicer than Google Calendar and don't care about Outlook.
Only use Notion Calendar (Cron) if you just need a calendar; stick with Todoist for managing personal tasks, but skip it for business.

Todoist is built around task management, letting you create, prioritize, and organize tasks in a straightforward way. Its calendar view and time blocking are afterthoughts, so if you're relying on it for daily planning or time blocking, you'll end up frustrated or needing to patch things together with other apps.

Notion Calendar (Cron) is all about the calendar experience. It's fast, beautifully designed, and nails the details for scheduling events, but it's not built for managing tasks at all. Trying to use it as a task manager gets messy fast, and even the integration with Notion databases doesn't fix that.

If you want to manage tasks, Todoist actually does the job, even if it's basic. For pure calendar scheduling, Notion Calendar is the clear pick and feels great to use. Don't try to stretch either one into what it isn't. Go with Todoist for tasks, Notion Calendar for events.

Notion Calendar gives you a nicer looking calendar for iOS if you just want something free and simpler than Google Calendar, but it is not available for Outlook and gets messy if you try to use it for tasks.
Only use Google Calendar if you need something for business or as a base for other calendar tools, since it is the standard everyone builds on.

If you care most about having a clean, fast, and thoughtfully designed calendar interface, Notion Calendar leaves Google Calendar in the dust. Every detail in Notion Calendar feels intentional, from its minimal look to its smooth keyboard shortcuts and flexible multi-day views. Even little touches like horizontal scrolling and clever workarounds for shared event editing show that the design team sweated the details.

Google Calendar's interface, on the other hand, is built for billions and it shows. It's cluttered, feels generic, and hasn't really changed in years. It's reliable, but you'll never call it delightful or enjoyable to use.

If you just want a calendar that feels great every time you open it, grab Notion Calendar. If you need the universal, never-surprising foundation that works everywhere and integrates with Google's ecosystem, Google Calendar is fine, but you'll be sacrificing design and day-to-day enjoyment. For pure user experience, Notion Calendar is the clear pick.
Notion Calendar gives you a cleaner, free calendar experience than Fantastical, but skip using it for managing tasks or Notion database items.
Only use Fantastical if you have very basic family needs, since Notion Calendar is the better pick for almost everyone else.

Notion Calendar stands out for its thoughtfully designed, fast, and responsive interface, with micro-interactions and keyboard shortcuts that make day-to-day use feel polished and efficient. Even though it pushes you toward the Notion ecosystem, the actual calendar experience is modern, smooth, and genuinely pleasant. It's free for individuals and students, making it super easy to recommend if you want a better-looking, more user-friendly upgrade over Google Calendar.

Fantastical, on the other hand, feels dated and doesn't keep up with newer options. It's not recommended for professional use or even for personal calendars, especially since there are more modern and capable alternatives out there. Plus, you have to pay after a short trial, which is a tough sell when the experience itself isn't keeping up.

If you care about having a calendar that feels current, sharp, and enjoyable to use, Notion Calendar is the clear pick. Fantastical just doesn't compete in terms of design, usability, or value. Go with Notion Calendar.

Vimcal is better if you're looking for a sleek and fast calendar, and want a way to schedule meetings with your team while on-the-go. Notion Calendar is better for those who are already using Notion since you can add database items to your calendar (because... why not I suppose? 😅)
That said, none of these tools are our top picks, there's some awesome calendars in our
best calendar apps
list.

Notion Calendar (Cron) stands out for its clean, responsive design and straightforward user experience. Every detail feels intentional, and it manages to deliver powerful features like keyboard shortcuts and smooth multi-timezone support without ever feeling overwhelming. Even small touches, like the customizable multi-day views and clever workarounds for editing shared events, show a focus on making things feel easy and pleasant.

Vimcal, on the other hand, tries to impress with a ton of keyboard shortcuts and specialized scheduling tools, especially for executive assistants handling complex, multi-timezone meetings. But this comes at a cost: the interface feels over-engineered and can get confusing fast. Even its natural language event creation, which should be a highlight, is inconsistent and sometimes frustrating to use. Instead of making things simpler, Vimcal often leaves you cleaning up after features that don't quite land.

If you want a calendar that just works, feels great, and doesn't get in your way, Notion Calendar (Cron) is the better pick. Only go with Vimcal if your main job is wrangling impossible schedules across time zones for executives, otherwise, it's just not worth the hassle. For everyone else, Notion Calendar nails the basics and feels delightful to use.

Motion and Notion Calendar can't seriously be compared. Notion Calendar is just a basic calendar that gives you the ability to add Notion database items to your calendar as to-dos. Motion on the other hand is a full-blown project management tool.

If you want a calendar that actively manages your time and tasks for you, Motion is in a league of its own. Its AI auto-scheduling doesn't just block out your day, it constantly rearranges tasks if you miss something, flags when you're at risk of missing deadlines, and adapts to team schedules. You show up every day knowing exactly what to work on next, and it keeps you honest about what's actually possible with your available time. This is for people who want their calendar to do more than just display events, it's a full assistant for busy professionals and teams who need everything in one place.

Notion Calendar (Cron) is all about the feel and flow of a classic calendar. The UI is gorgeous, it's fast, and the shortcuts make it a pleasure to use. If you just want a beautifully designed, free calendar that's better than Google Calendar for event scheduling and viewing, this is the clear pick. But it won't manage your workload, reschedule tasks, or help you keep up with project deadlines. It's not interested in being a task manager or productivity engine.

If your priority is a calendar that actually manages your time, keeps you on track with tasks, and helps you avoid falling behind, Motion is the obvious choice. If you only need a clean, fast, and thoughtfully designed calendar for events, Notion Calendar is the better fit.

items.name: Todoist
items.description: A to-do list to organize your work & life.

docs.name: 2 Free Months of Pro
docs.terms: Get 2 free months of Todoist Pro by using the link above.

children.text: Todoist covers the basics for personal task management with no extra features, while Trello feels outdated unless you need Atlassian integrations or a simple board.
Only use Trello if your team relies on Atlassian tools; otherwise, Todoist is the more practical pick for straightforward personal tasks.

Todoist is built for straightforward personal task management, letting you create, prioritize, and organize tasks in a list, kanban board, or (if you pay) a basic calendar view. It nails the basics for solo users, but doesn't do much else. If you want to get into time blocking, Todoist's calendar view is there, but it's minimal and really just tacked on. For anything beyond simple task lists, you'll need to integrate with another daily planner to fill in the gaps.

Trello, on the other hand, is all about kanban boards. If you love dragging cards around and want a clean board view, Trello still does that well. It also has a solid mobile app and deep integrations with Atlassian tools like Jira and Confluence. But outside of those integrations and the board view, Trello hasn't kept up with newer project managers. Its feature set feels stuck in the past, and it's not built for deep personal productivity or more advanced planning.

If your main concern is classic task management for your personal life, Todoist is the better pick. It's focused, reliable, and does what you expect without extra clutter. Trello is only worth considering if you specifically want a kanban board above all else or need those Atlassian integrations. Otherwise, Todoist is the clear choice for simple, effective personal task tracking.

app.name: Todoist
app.description: A to-do list to organize your work & life.

children.text: Todoist keeps task management simple and practical for personal use, while Routine gets confusing by trying to do too much with planning and databases.
Only use Routine if you really need its attempt at connecting knowledge and projects, but Todoist is the clear pick for straightforward daily task handling.

Todoist is straightforward and reliable for personal task management. You get predictable basics like creating, prioritizing, and organizing tasks, plus simple list, kanban, and calendar views. There's no confusion about what it does or how to use it. Time blocking is there, but it's minimal, so if you want deep daily planning, you'll probably end up using another app alongside it.

Routine tries to offer everything at once, daily planning, project management, Notion-style databases, but ends up feeling messy and confusing. The interface is clunky, basic actions take trial and error, and it's way too easy to break things you shouldn't be able to touch. Even though it has a free tier and lets you drag tasks onto a calendar, the experience is neither smooth nor clear. Instead of making your life easier, it adds mental load and complexity, especially with its labels and note features that just muddy the workflow.

If you want a simple, no-nonsense task manager for personal use, Todoist is the clear pick. Routine's ambition gets in its own way, leading to a confusing and unstable experience, while Todoist just works. Only go with Routine if you're desperate for something free and don't mind wrestling with clunky design, otherwise, stick with Todoist.

Todoist handles basic personal task management and works if you just want to keep track of to-dos, but Reclaim AI is better if you need to block out time for focus and breaks on Google Calendar and mostly deal with internal meetings, not detailed tasks or external scheduling.
Only use Todoist if you want simple personal task lists; choose Reclaim AI if your main problem is defending focus time from internal meetings on Google Calendar.

Todoist gives you straightforward task management with lists, priorities, labels, and a couple of basic views. You get exactly what you expect: you can organize your own tasks, see them in a list or kanban, and, if you pay, use a barebones calendar view. Time blocking is possible but feels tacked on and limited. If you want to actually manage a bunch of tasks for yourself, Todoist covers the basics without much fuss, but don't expect advanced planning or anything beyond simple organization.

Reclaim AI, on the other hand, isn't really a task manager. It's built for people constantly stuck in meetings who need to carve out time for focus work and habits on their Google Calendar. You can enter some tasks, but the task features are minimal, just priority and deadline, with no real project management, collaboration, or big-picture task tracking. Reclaim expects you to use a separate task manager for anything beyond that.

If you care about managing tasks, adding them, organizing them, seeing what's next, and actually handling your to-do list, Todoist is the only real option here. Reclaim is for defending calendar space, not managing actual tasks. Go with Todoist if you want to get your tasks under control. Use Reclaim only if your main pain is having no focus time because of nonstop meetings, not if you need to actually manage your work.
Todoist handles personal task management reliably and simply, while Notion tries to cover more but only really works well for team docs.
Only use Todoist for managing your own tasks; stick with Notion only if you specifically need a shared team knowledge base.

Todoist is straightforward for personal task management. You get simple task lists, basic prioritization, and some limited views. If your goal is just to keep track of your own to-dos or personal projects, Todoist does the job without any setup or learning curve.

Notion, on the other hand, tries to be a customizable workspace and can technically act as a task manager if you're willing to build out your own system. But that flexibility turns into a headache for most people. You'll spend more time setting up, maintaining, and explaining your task system than actually using it. Teams especially run into problems with adoption and workflow chaos, since the structure only makes sense to whoever built it.

If you need task management that just works out of the box and doesn't get in the way, Todoist is the clear pick. Notion is only worth considering if you want to build something highly customized and are prepared for ongoing work to keep it usable, but for most people and teams, that quickly becomes more trouble than it's worth.

Todoist gives you straightforward personal task management with no extras, while Morgen Calendar offers a faster interface and lets you add simple tasks but is mainly a calendar upgrade rather than a true task manager like Todoist.
Only use Morgen Calendar if you want a better calendar and just need basic tasks; stick with Todoist for actual task management.

Todoist is built specifically for task management and gives you all the basics you'd expect: creating, prioritizing, and organizing tasks with labels, plus list, kanban, and (on paid plans) a simple calendar view. Its focus is clear: if you need to manage tasks, especially for personal life, Todoist does it directly and reliably. While its calendar and time-blocking features are minimal, you still get more task management depth than with Morgen.

Morgen Calendar, on the other hand, is a calendar first. It looks and feels better than Google Calendar or Outlook and lets you add very simple tasks, just a name and deadline, then drag them onto your schedule. But you can't label tasks, categorize them, or manage bigger projects from within Morgen. If all you want is to dabble in daily planning and occasionally toss a task onto your calendar, Morgen feels smooth and easy. But anyone with more than a handful of tasks, or who cares about organizing or tracking them, will quickly hit its limits.

If task management is what matters, Todoist is the clear pick. Morgen only works if your needs are extremely basic and you care more about calendar design than actually managing tasks. For anything beyond dabbling, Todoist simply gives you more control and organizational tools.
Todoist covers personal task management simply and reliably, while Linear focuses on project management for engineers and is unmatched there.
Only use Todoist if you want a straightforward tool for your personal life; choose Linear if you need something purpose built for engineering projects.

Linear is built specifically for engineers and product teams, tying together bugs, feature requests, and sprints in a way that makes it the clear favorite for those workflows. If you're in an engineering environment, nothing matches how well Linear fits that process. Teams genuinely enjoy using it, and it's widely advocated by those who rely on it for product management.

Todoist, on the other hand, is practical for personal task management and daily life, but its business and team features are limited. It doesn't offer the same depth for collaboration, project management, or engineering workflows, and even its calendar and time blocking are minimal compared to what's needed for more complex team needs.

If your main goal is managing engineering projects or product cycles, Linear is the obvious pick. Todoist is fine for personal lists, but it just can't compete with Linear's focus and depth for technical teams. If you're not an engineer or don't need that level of project structure, Todoist is simple and straightforward, but for anything serious on the engineering side, Linear is the one to use.
Todoist handles personal task management reliably and simply, while Amie now focuses on being an AI notetaker instead of to-dos.
Only use Todoist if you want straightforward personal task lists; choose Amie if you mainly need an AI notetaker without a meeting bot.

Todoist is focused on personal task management and gives you straightforward tools to create, prioritize, and organize tasks. Its calendar view and time blocking are basic, but if your main need is reliable, practical task tracking for your own life, Todoist does the job cleanly without distractions.

Amie, on the other hand, has shifted away from being a calendar and to-do app. Its priority is now AI meeting notes and automation, not core task management. The task and calendar features are still there, but they aren't the focus anymore.

If you care about actual task management, Todoist is the clear pick. Amie only makes sense if your primary goal is meeting transcription and follow-up, not managing your tasks. For anything resembling classic to-do list needs, Todoist delivers what you expect, while Amie has moved on to something else entirely.
Akiflow lets you pull in tasks from tools like ClickUp, Asana, Slack, and email to one place and time block them on your calendar, which Todoist does not.
Only use Todoist if you just need a straightforward personal task list and do not care about integrations or calendar time blocking like in Akiflow.

If you want true time blocking, Akiflow is the clear pick. It lets you drag tasks directly onto your calendar and forces you to actively plan when things will happen, giving you full control. Todoist's time blocking is an afterthought and feels pretty limited, so much so that people actually use Akiflow or Sunsama alongside Todoist just to get a proper daily planning workflow.

Akiflow's approach is hands-on and manual, so it's perfect if you like to intentionally map out your day and see everything from all your connected apps in one spot. Just know you'll need to keep up with your planning or things can get messy fast. Todoist, meanwhile, is solid for simple task management but falls flat if you care about scheduling your work on a calendar.

If you're serious about time blocking and want to consolidate tasks from a bunch of tools into one place, go with Akiflow. If you just need basic lists and light organization, Todoist is fine, but don't expect much from its time blocking.

The user experience for Typefully is 100x better than Hootsuite for posting, so if you're deciding between the two for X or LinkedIn,
Typefully is the clear winner.

Typefully makes scheduling posts on X and LinkedIn straightforward and actually enjoyable, with a smooth experience and focused features for writing, drafting, and scheduling content. If your main platforms are X or LinkedIn and you care about efficiency and analytics, Typefully is the clear choice.

Hootsuite, on the other hand, feels outdated and clunky. The interface is overwhelming, especially for small businesses or solo users, and most of its advanced features only unlock at the expensive enterprise tier. Unless you're managing social media for a huge company with complex approval workflows, Hootsuite just gets in your way.

If you want fast, easy scheduling and analytics for X or LinkedIn, go with Typefully every time. Only consider Hootsuite if you're at a large enterprise that absolutely needs layered permissions and deep reporting, and even then, the experience is still a slog. For almost everyone else, skip Hootsuite.

The user experience for Typefully is much better than Metricool for posting, so if you're deciding between the two for X or LinkedIn,

Typefully is hands down the better option if your main focus is posting on X or LinkedIn. The review makes it clear: if X or LinkedIn are your main channels, stick with Typefully. Metricool just doesn't deliver for those platforms.

items.name: Cal.com
items.description: Open scheduling infrastructure for everyone.

children.text: Cal.com handles complex scheduling tasks like payments and round robin booking, while Google Calendar is the base for most calendar tools but lacks these extras.
Only use Cal.com if you need advanced scheduling; stick with Google Calendar for basic business calendar needs or if you plan to use another tool on top.

If you need serious scheduling firepower like round robin assignments, payment collection during booking, or automated follow-ups, Cal.com blows Google Calendar out of the water. Cal.com is made for people and teams with complex scheduling needs and lets you build out custom workflows, handle team availability, and automate communication before and after meetings. It's the choice for sales teams, support, startups, or anyone who wants their scheduler to do more than just book a time.

Google Calendar is great as the universal backbone for everyday events, reminders, and basic calendar sharing, especially if you already live in the Google ecosystem. But when it comes to advanced scheduling, it's just not built for that kind of flexibility or depth.

If all you want is a straightforward calendar to manage your own appointments, Google Calendar is fine and free. But if you actually care about scheduling as a workflow, especially for teams or anything where you need automation or payment collection, Cal.com is the only real option here.

app.name: Cal.com
app.description: Open scheduling infrastructure for everyone.

children.text: Cal.com handles complex scheduling like payments and round robin, while Fantastical is mainly for personal or family calendars.
Only use Cal.com if you need advanced scheduling features; skip Fantastical since better options exist for both personal and professional use.

Cal.com completely outclasses Fantastical when it comes to scheduling for professionals or teams. Cal.com is built for complex scheduling needs like round robin assignments, automated follow-ups, and payments on booking, and it's designed to handle everything from solo use up to enterprise workflows. It even lets technical users tap into a robust API or use it as infrastructure for their own products.

Fantastical, on the other hand, isn't recommended for professional use at all. It's described as outdated and lacking the power or flexibility needed for modern scheduling, even on a personal level. If you want something that works for individuals, teams, startups, or enterprises, especially if you need anything beyond basic calendar features, Cal.com is the clear choice. Fantastical just doesn't measure up for anything but the simplest personal calendars, and even then, there are better options.
Cal.com handles complex scheduling needs like payments and automated follow-ups, while Vimcal is only about booking meetings quickly.
Only use Vimcal if scheduling meetings faster is all you care about; choose Cal.com for anything more involved.

Cal.com handles complex scheduling needs way better than Vimcal. If you need to take payments when someone books, set up automated pre or post meeting reminders, or run round robin scheduling for a sales or support team, Cal.com is built for that from the ground up. It's the go-to for anyone who wants maximum flexibility, whether you're an individual, startup, or a big team juggling lots of moving parts.

Vimcal is great if you're an executive assistant or someone scheduling tons of big meetings, especially if you love fast group polling and a snappy iOS interface. But it doesn't handle payments, round robin, or advanced workflows. It's really just about speed and making life easier for people who constantly wrangle group meetings.

If you want the most capable scheduler for anything beyond basic meeting coordination, pick Cal.com every time. Only go with Vimcal if your main headache is polling big groups for times and you're locked into iOS. Otherwise, Cal.com blows it away for complex scheduling.
Cal.com handles complex scheduling like payments and automated follow-ups, while TidyCal only matches basic features on Cal.com's free tier.
Only use TidyCal if you already paid for it, otherwise pick Cal.com for anything beyond barebones scheduling.

Cal.com completely outclasses TidyCal on scheduling features and flexibility. Cal.com covers everything TidyCal offers for free, then goes way further with advanced scheduling options like payments on booking, round robin distribution, and automated follow-ups.

TidyCal's only real advantage used to be its one-time price, but Cal.com's free tier now gives you the same scheduling basics without the upfront cost. If you need anything beyond the most basic scheduling, or care about ongoing updates and improvements, Cal.com is the obvious pick.

Unless you're looking for the cheapest possible tool and don't care about future development, there's no real reason to pick TidyCal over Cal.com. Cal.com is just plain better for anyone who cares about modern scheduling.
Cal.com handles complex scheduling like payments and automated follow ups, while SavvyCal misses the workflow depth for heavy needs.
Only use SavvyCal if you want a simple, personal scheduling interface and do not need advanced features found in Cal.com.

Cal.com blows SavvyCal out of the water for complex scheduling needs. If you need things like round robin assignment, payments during booking, recurring appointments, or any kind of automated pre- and post-meeting workflow, Cal.com is the only real choice. Its flexibility and API options make it fit for everything from solo users to enterprises, and it's the only one here that can actually support technical integrations or custom scheduling infrastructure.

SavvyCal feels friendlier for basic, personal scheduling and has a nice interface with features like letting invitees drag to pick times, but it just can't handle heavy or complicated scheduling. There's no deep workflow or API support, so it doesn't scale beyond simple team meetings.

Bottom line: If you care about advanced scheduling, workflows, or need anything more than the basics, pick Cal.com every time. SavvyCal only makes sense if you want a slightly nicer interface for lightweight, personal scheduling and don't need any serious features.
Cal.com handles complex scheduling needs and works for a wide range of users, while Chili Piper is only built for enterprise sales teams on Salesforce.
Only use Chili Piper if you run an enterprise sales team on Salesforce; use Cal.com for everything else or if you want more flexibility.

Cal.com blows Chili Piper out of the water when it comes to flexibility and modern scheduling features. Cal lets you handle everything from payments on booking to custom workflows and has the strongest round robin scheduling around. It supports a wide range of users, from individuals to enterprises, and works no matter what CRM or calendar setup you have.

Chili Piper is all about big enterprise sales teams that live in Salesforce. If that's your world and you need deep Salesforce integration with round robin booking for leads, Chili Piper fits. But if you want anything beyond that, or you're not using Salesforce, Chili Piper just doesn't have the flexibility.

Unless you're a Salesforce-heavy enterprise sales org, Cal.com is the clear choice. It adapts to more workflows, supports more teams, and gives you way more control. Chili Piper is only worth considering if Salesforce is non-negotiable and you don't care about flexibility for anything else.
If you're looking for the best scheduling tool on the market,
Cal.com
is it, hands-down. If you are just scheduling meetings and don't need robust routing capabilities, collecting payments to book, and more, then
Motion
will do what you need it to.
That said, Motion does not have an API for its scheduler capabilities, so you're restricted to just basic meeting booking with Motion.

Cal.com blows Motion out of the water for complex scheduling needs. If you need round robin scheduling, lead routing, automated pre- and post-call workflows, or want to take payments directly when someone books, Cal.com is built for that level of control. It handles everything from individual bookings to multi-person team scheduling and recurring appointments, and it's flexible enough for enterprises, startups, and even technical users who want to build custom flows or integrate with other systems.

Motion's scheduler is fine if you just want to share your availability or set up simple booking pages, especially if you're already using it for project management. But it falls short for anything beyond basic scheduling. There's no lead routing, no payment processing, and it can't automate complex workflows. The focus is on integrating simple scheduling into your task and calendar system, not on handling serious scheduling operations.

If your main concern is powerful, flexible scheduling with advanced features, pick Cal.com every time. Motion is only worth considering if you already use it for managing tasks and your scheduling needs are minimal. For anyone who actually cares about robust scheduling, Cal.com is the clear winner.
While Calendly was the leader the the meeting scheduling space for the past decade, over the past few years Cal.com has given them a run for their money, and in our opinion, has has overtaken them.

We've implemented Calendly into numerous businesses over the years as it used to be our top recommendation for any team that had deep scheduling needs... but after evaluating Cal.com, we can confidently say that Cal.com is much more modern and will unlock more time savings and potential for any business that has robust scheduling needs.

If you're a business that wants to have full control of your booking process (from asking questions users prior to scheduling), take payments, route meetings to the right people on your team, and have ample of customized automations pre-and-post calls, Cal.com is going to seriously impress you. Oh, and you can
try it for free

Cal.com completely outpaces Calendly on flexibility and handling complex scheduling needs. If you care about things like round robin scheduling, automated follow-ups, taking payments during booking, or creating custom workflows, Cal.com is the clear pick. It's built for individuals, teams, and even larger organizations that need to coordinate across multiple calendars or set up more advanced booking flows.

Calendly used to be the standard, but now it feels old and limited, especially for teams who want fast performance and adaptable workflows. If you just want a basic meeting scheduler and don't need much customization, you could get by with Calendly or even a bundled tool from your existing subscriptions. But the minute you want anything beyond simple bookings, Calendly falls behind.

If scheduling is core to your workflow and you want modern features or flexibility, go with Cal.com every time. Only stick with Calendly if you don't care about advanced options and just need something basic. Cal.com is the obvious choice for anyone who wants more than the bare minimum.

children.text: Motion builds your schedule automatically using AI to organize tasks, projects, and meetings, while Otter just transcribes calls and meetings live.
Only use Otter if you need quick live transcription for personal stuff; pick Motion if you want AI to manage your workday and tasks.

Motion's meeting recorder is tightly integrated with its project management features, letting you automatically turn meeting notes into actionable tasks and link everything back to your projects. This means after a call, you get organized notes, instant task creation, and all your documentation stays connected without extra steps.

Otter is much simpler. It's great for just recording or transcribing a conversation, like at a doctor's appointment or for personal reference, but it stops there. It doesn't handle business workflows or tie notes back to projects, so you end up with a pile of transcripts you have to manage yourself.

If you want meeting notes that actually drive your work forward and cut out manual follow-up, Motion is the clear pick for business teams. Otter is only worth considering if all you need is a basic recording or transcription tool for personal use.

app.name: Motion
app.description: Use AI to plan your work, automatically. Be 137% more productive.

children.text: Motion builds your day with AI by organizing tasks, deadlines, and meetings into your calendar, while Gong analyzes sales calls and emails to show what's working in enterprise sales.
Only use Gong if you run a large sales team with complex deals; use Motion if you want AI to organize all your work, not just sales.

Motion blows Gong out of the water when it comes to actually managing projects and automating your day-to-day work. Motion takes all your tasks, meetings, and deadlines, then actively schedules and reschedules everything for you, so you spend almost zero time babysitting your workflow. The AI isn't just a side feature, it's baked into every part of how you get work done, from project setup to meeting notes that instantly become tasks.

Gong, on the other hand, is all about analyzing sales activity for big sales teams. It records calls, emails, and meetings, then runs AI to flag risks and suggest next steps in deals. But it's not a project manager and doesn't handle task scheduling or daily workflow at all. If you're not running a large sales team, Gong's features don't help with general project management or personal productivity.

So if you need a tool that will keep your projects moving automatically and handle the busy work for you, Motion is the clear pick. Gong is only worth considering if your entire focus is sales process analysis at an enterprise level. For anything related to project management, Motion is miles ahead.

Motion acts like a personal assistant by using AI to organize your tasks, projects, and schedule, while Fireflies feels cluttered and buggy unless you really need advanced meeting audio analysis and love sorting soundbites.
Only use Fireflies if you specifically want deep meeting audio tools and don't mind complexity; stick with Motion for a more streamlined, AI-driven workday.

Motion nails meeting recording for teams who want a recorder that just works in the background and automatically ties meeting notes, tasks, and project details together. Its meeting recorder joins your calls, records everything, then uses AI to organize notes and even creates actionable tasks you can assign right away. You barely have to touch it, and it connects directly to your ongoing projects so all your meeting content is immediately useful and in context.

Fireflies, on the other hand, piles on a ton of meeting recording features and deep integrations, but the experience is overwhelming unless you're the kind of person or team who loves manually organizing call snippets and playlists. Its audio recording was ahead of its time, but now, unless you're in a niche that needs advanced audio analysis or you're religious about tagging and sorting soundbites, it just adds stress and clutter. Even basic video recording is locked behind the top pricing tier, and you constantly run into upsells and storage limits.

If you want a meeting recorder that disappears into your workflow and helps your team get actual work done with minimal effort, Motion is the clear pick. Only go with Fireflies if you specifically need those unique audio snippet tools and are okay managing the added complexity. For most teams, Motion is simply easier, more integrated, and far less hassle.
Motion actually feels like having a personal assistant that builds your day using your team's projects, tasks, and deadlines, while Cluely is mostly known for its marketing and feels less finished.
Only use Cluely if you want something unique and don't mind rough edges, but Motion gets you working faster and just works better day to day.

Motion actually handles meeting notes and documentation in a way that feels built-in and useful for project management. Its AI Docs and Meeting Recorder don't just capture what was said, they instantly tie notes and tasks back to your projects, so action items never get lost and you can see everything in one place. Tasks are created automatically from meetings, and you can approve or assign them without extra steps.

Cluely, in contrast, is mostly an AI chat overlay that suggests questions and context during calls. It's active and persistent, but it doesn't integrate meeting notes or documentation back into a broader project workflow. Its real advantage is surfacing info in the moment, but once the call's over, you're left with a transcript and some notes, not a living record that drives your next steps.

If you care about meeting notes and docs that actually move your projects forward, Motion is the clear pick. Cluely's approach is more about real-time suggestions, but it doesn't match Motion's depth or integration for capturing, connecting, and acting on meeting outputs.
Motion organizes all your projects and tasks with AI that builds your day and acts like a true assistant, while tl;dv just handles meeting notes and sharing during calls.
Only use tl;dv if you specifically need live collaborative call notes with Slack integration, otherwise pick Motion for full work management.

If you want your day planned for you, with tasks and projects automatically scheduled onto your calendar so you never have to think about what comes next, Motion is the clear pick. Its whole approach is about removing manual planning and mental overhead, giving you a mapped-out workday the moment you log in. The AI handles all the busywork, so you just show up and execute without fussing over details or rearranging your schedule.

If your main need is real-time, collaborative meeting note-taking with instant sharing and Slack integration during calls, tl;dv does that really well. It lets everyone see and contribute to meeting notes as they happen, making the process feel seamless inside meetings. But outside of calls and collaborative note-taking, it doesn't give you the same hands-off, automated day planning that Motion does.

For actually getting work scheduled and executed without manual effort, Motion wins. If you care most about live note-taking in meetings, tl;dv is strong, but for full-day productivity and removing all the guesswork from your workflow, Motion is the one to pick.

Motion is a full blown task and project management software that has calendar at the core. It uses AI to auto-schedule tasks on your calendar. TickTick is a barebones todo list app that is better used for personal and more basic tasks. Their calendar integration is also incredibly inconsistent and lacking.

If you want a tool that actually manages your time for you and keeps you ahead of your deadlines, Motion is in a different league. It doesn't just track your tasks, it actively schedules them onto your calendar, automatically shuffles things when your plans change, and warns you if you're about to miss a deadline. You get instant updates and the whole point is to stop wasting time fiddling with your task list.

TickTick is just a basic to-do list. It's fine if you just want to jot down a few personal tasks and don't care about anything beyond that, but you're on your own when it comes to actually managing your schedule or reacting to shifting priorities. Even the calendar sync is slow, so you won't see real-time changes.

For anyone who needs actual help with task management and wants to work smarter, Motion does the work for you. Pick TickTick only if you want something super simple and don't mind doing all the planning and rescheduling yourself.

Jira is for enterprise engineering teams (1000 employees +) who work with traditional project management methodologies like scrum and agile, while Motion is a newer project management tool for SMBs (1–200 employees) that has a calendar at the core, and uses AI to auto-schedule work on every team members calendar.
If you're a small business looking for an all-in-one solution, Motion is as if GoHighLevel was actually built in the AI era.
While HighLevel has more overall features, they are quite kludgy, and Motion is building just the most important features in the right way.
No question that Motion is going to beat HighLevel in this area especially over the months to come.
Read our full
GoHighLevel review
to fully understand the major concerns we have about GoHighLevel.

The concept around the software is a good idea in theory: the only tool you need for your SMB. That said, you can tell most of the "features" that they built are often half-baked just to check the box, and the entire UI/UX feels like it's out of the 2000's, with AI tacked on just so they can say they "use AI".
That's where we're way more bullish on
Motion
to actually build the all-in-one SMB workspace, as they're building way more purposefully, with AI built-in at the core. The user experience between the two software are night and day.
We'd highly recommend giving Motion a shot, and using a light-weight solution for
CRM
and
Website Builder
, until Motion inevitably builds this directly into the product in the months to come. If you're an SMB, you'll certainly be happy you went this route over the GoHighLevel path.

Many praise Motion for it's AI features to help you with task and time management. But just like any app, it's not for everyone. This article will cover the best motion alternatives so you can find the best app for you.
First, What is Motion?

Motion is a project and task manger that uses AI to help you plan your workload, schedule your meetings and automatically build your to-do list based on your projects. It's great for teams 1-50 that are looking for an effective way to manage their projects and get more done faster (and spend less time planning their day).
Why Motion Alternatives?

If you're comparing the best Motion alternatives, you first want to ask yourself which category you're looking to replace. Is it project management? Task management? A meeting scheduler? Since Motion does all of these things, it's important to categorize what features are most important to you that your replacement tool needs to have.
Here are some common reasons why you may be looking for Motion alternatives:
You don't want AI to help you plan your day. Some folks prefer a more mindful/manual way of planning and time blocking their day (
hint: make sure to read the
Sunsama review
if this is you
You want a more more robust project management tool. While we think Motion is the
best project management tool
, some teams need more features than Motion offers, like different project views, reporting, dashboards, or advanced features.
Motion is out of budget. While we think for professionals, Motion is worth the price for how much time it saves us with project management, for students or those just starting out, maybe a
free daily planner
would suffice.
If you're just using the Motion meeting scheduler, you're only using a small part of the tools features. In this case, the best Motion alternative for you would be a tool that only does
calendar scheduling
Automatic Task Scheduling Alternatives

The thing is, if you're looking for something more like a daily planner that automatically schedules tasks on your calendar for you, your best bet is going to be Motion. That said, if you're here, you're specifically asking for an alternative. That's where things are tough because well, not many Motion alternatives actually schedule tasks automatically on your calendar.
The problem with daily planner apps (talking about having tasks on your calendar) are all going the path of "mindful time-blocking", AKA: manually drag tasks on your calendar from other apps.
Or then there's a tool like
Reclaim
that auto-schedules tasks, but it's incredibly barebones for actually managing your tasks (it's not a proper project manager or task management tool), and you need to still continue using
Google Calendar
as it doesn't actually have full calendar functionality, it's really more read-only.

So if you're looking for automatic task scheduling, you're going to be stuck with Motion as the front-runner, if you don't mind not having auto-scheduling tasks, keep reading to dive in deeper and learn about all the best Motion alternatives!

items.name: Slite
items.description: Slite is a shared team knowledge-base platform for teams of all sizes—document, communicate, make decisions, and work asynchronously.
