children.text: Slite keeps team docs and decisions clear and easy to use, while Superlist looks nice but is buggy and unreliable for collaboration.
Only use Superlist if you care more about fun design and animations than actually getting team work done; stick with Slite for anything serious.

Slite is miles ahead when it comes to team collaboration and shared documentation. Everything about its design keeps teams focused, organized, and free from the clutter and distraction you'd get in more complex tools. The interface is clean, it's basically impossible to make a document look bad, and you never get lost or sidetracked with formatting. Teams can get in, find what they need, make decisions, and move on fast. Collaboration just works, and the whole experience feels reliable and smooth.

Superlist, on the other hand, shines for personal task management and individual organization, with a beautiful interface and playful touches like satisfying completion sounds. But its team features just aren't ready. Even basic collaboration is buggy, and it's unreliable even for simple shared tasks between two people. If your goal is to work together as a team, especially for anything beyond simple shared lists, Superlist will frustrate you.
If you care about easy, dependable team collaboration and shared knowledge, pick Slite every time. Superlist is lovely for solo use, but it's not built for real team workflows yet.

app.name: Slite
app.description: Slite is a shared team knowledge-base platform for teams of all sizes—document, communicate, make decisions, and work asynchronously.

children.text: Slite gives you a clean, focused space for team documentation and decisions without the clutter you get from Coda.
Only use Coda if you want to experiment or build custom tools; stick with Slite for straightforward, reliable team knowledge sharing.

Slite nails clarity and simplicity for team documentation. You can jump in, find what you need, and contribute without getting lost in formatting or endless options. The interface stays clean, documents always look good, and your team won't waste time figuring out where things are or how to keep docs consistent.

Coda is more about flexibility and building custom workflows, but when it comes to serving as a team knowledge base, it gets messy. Switching between documents feels clunky, like digging through Google Drive, and searching across everything is a pain. Coda's shift toward no-code tools means the documentation experience is now an afterthought, not the main event.

If you care most about having a straightforward, focused, and reliable space for shared documentation that your team will actually use, Slite is the clear pick. Coda's strengths are for people who want to build custom tools, not those who just want their team knowledge base to work without drama.
Slite keeps docs and decisions clear and distraction free, while Notion gets cluttered when you try to use it for more than documentation.
Only use Notion if you must have an all-in-one suite, but for clean team knowledge bases, stick with Slite.

Slite keeps things simple and clean, so your team never gets bogged down in setup or endless formatting. You can jump in, collaborate, and actually find what you need without distractions or the risk of making documentation messy. The UI stays consistent, and there's no way to make a doc look bad, so even non-technical teams get a reliable, straightforward knowledge base with zero confusion.

Notion, on the other hand, gives you a ton of flexibility, but that's exactly where most teams get stuck. It looks tempting at first, but you end up spending more time building systems, tweaking layouts, and trying to keep everyone on the same page. Unless you have a technical team ready to put in the work, adoption drops off fast and the structure only makes sense to the person who set it up.

If your main goal is a knowledge base that just works for the whole team with no hassle, Slite is the clear pick. If you want to tinker or need something deeply custom, Notion has the tools, but for most teams, that extra complexity just gets in the way of actually using your documentation.

items.name: Stable
items.description: A permanent address and virtual mailbox to scan mail, forward packages, deposit checks, and more, for teams of all sizes.

docs.name: Get 50% off (1 year)
docs.terms: New accounts only, use promo code "efficient" at checkout

items.name: Dub Links
items.description: Dub is the modern link attribution platform for you to create short links, track conversions, and run affiliate programs.

children.text: Dub Links gives you real-time tracking, reliable analytics, and clear dashboards, while Short.io only handles basic redirects and has messy reporting.
Only use Short.io if you have no other option; choose Dub Links for much better tracking, analytics, and usability.

Dub Links absolutely crushes Short.io when it comes to analytics and reporting. Dub's dashboard is clear, modern, and actually makes it easy to get accurate, helpful link tracking and conversion data. Marketers and anyone serious about understanding link performance will find Dub's real-time tracking and deep analytics a massive upgrade.

Short.io barely covers the basics, its reporting is described as a mess, to the point that users just avoid it entirely. It's only "adequate" for simple redirects, not for anyone who wants usable analytics.
If you care about analytics, there's no contest. Go with Dub Links. Short.io doesn't even come close.

app.name: Dub Links
app.description: Dub is the modern link attribution platform for you to create short links, track conversions, and run affiliate programs.

children.text: Dub Links gives you real-time tracking, reliable analytics, and a modern interface, while Bitly feels outdated and limits key features.
Only use Bitly if you have a specific reason, since Dub Links is just much easier and more useful day to day.

Dub Links completely outclasses Bitly when it comes to analytics and reporting. Dub's dashboard is minimal, clear, and gives you real-time link tracking with deep conversion data that's actually useful for marketers. You get features like QR codes, tags, password protection, expiring links, and geo-targeting all laid out in a way that makes sense.

Bitly's analytics are stuck in the past. The dashboard is clunky, pulling reports is a pain, and you don't get real-time updates. Important features like custom domains and meaningful tracking history are locked behind expensive paywalls, and if you're not paying, Bitly even runs ads against your links, which just looks bad.
If you care at all about reliable, easy-to-read analytics and reporting, Dub Links is the only real choice here. Bitly just doesn't keep up.

items.name: Screen Studio
items.description: High quality videos as easy as taking a screenshot (designed for macOS).

docs.displayName: Screen Studio - You're Getting The Best Deal
docs.name: You're Getting The Best Deal
docs.terms: You're getting the best deal, no really, I mean it... We tried getting you a deal and there is none. We paid full price as well!

children.text: Screen Studio nails high quality screen recordings for showing off product interfaces or posting slick GIFs and short videos, while CleanShot X focuses on quick screenshots, easy annotation, and keeping things organized.
Only use CleanShot X if you mostly take and edit screenshots; choose Screen Studio for standout screen recordings every time.

Screen Studio blows CleanShot X out of the water for screen recording quality, especially if you care about making your videos look sharp and engaging. With Screen Studio, your recordings get automatic zooms, smooth cursor movement, and edits that make everything feel polished right out of the box. You barely need to know anything about video editing, just record, maybe tweak a little, and you've got something that looks pro enough for product demos, social media, or promo videos. CleanShot X just can't compete here; its recordings feel flat and basic, with none of the built-in flair or auto-editing that makes Screen Studio's output pop.

If you're after impressive, attention-grabbing screen recordings for demos or content, Screen Studio is the clear pick. CleanShot X is great for screenshots and quick markups, but for video, it's not in the same league.

app.name: Screen Studio
app.description: High quality videos as easy as taking a screenshot (designed for macOS).

children.text: Screen Studio delivers the highest quality screen recordings for showing off product interfaces or posting short videos, while Loom has become bloated and frustrating to use.
Only use Loom if you have no other choice; Screen Studio is the one to pick for quality and ease.

Screen Studio blows past Loom when it comes to creating polished, professional screen recordings, especially for things like product demos, social media videos, and tutorials. Its automatic zoom, smooth cursor tracking, and instant editing make every video look sharp without any extra work. You get that "how did they make that?" effect right out of the box, and it's super easy to tweak for different platforms.

Loom used to be the go-to for quick team videos, but now it's bogged down with crashes, constant upgrade nags, and basic features. If you just need a quick free recording under five minutes, Loom's free tier is fine, but nothing about it stands out for high-quality or engaging content anymore.
If you want videos that look great and actually impress people, grab Screen Studio. Loom doesn't come close on quality or user experience for this.
It's tough to truly compare Tella vs Screen Studio because they are quite different—so different that we actually use both. We used Tella to film our
Motion Course
as it allowed us the flexibility to record our camera + screen and edit the video all within the tool. On the other hand, we use Screen Studio to show off software in most of our
YouTube videos
as it allows for beautiful zoom-ins.
Both Tella and Screen Studio have their strengths.
Screen Studio is slightly more high-end for doing screen recordings as they allow for beautiful zoom in's (Tella's zoom feature is much more basic).

If you are doing a high-end tutorial that you want to invest a bit more time into, we'd recommend Screen Studio to make it feel a bit more premium. That said, while Screen Studio does "automatic zooms", they still require some manual effort to get just right so expect to spend a bit of time tinkering with it.

On the other hand, Tella is better if you care less specifically about having fancy zoom-ins on yourscreen recordings, and want to capture multiple elements (like yourself on camera, along with the screen recording, on a nice custom background).

items.name: Spark
items.description: Smart. Focused. Email. Fast, cross-platform email designed to filter out the noise - so you can focus on what's important.

children.text: Spark gives you a faster and more organized email experience than Tatem, which is barely maintained and might stop working anytime.
Only use Tatem if you have a specific reason, but skip it for Spark, which is free and actually gets updates.

app.name: Spark
app.description: Smart. Focused. Email. Fast, cross-platform email designed to filter out the noise - so you can focus on what's important.

children.text: Spark gives you a faster and more organized email setup for free, while Skiff is shut down and can't be used at all.
Only use Spark since Skiff is no longer available.

Spark is the only real option here since Skiff has been shut down and is no longer available. Spark not only works, but also offers a cleaner, more organized email experience than Gmail or Outlook, especially for individuals and students who want something free and simple to set up. Skiff simply can't be recommended because you can't use it anymore. If you want an email client you can actually rely on, go with Spark.
Spark gives you a faster and more organized inbox than Outlook, especially if you want something free and easy as an individual or student.
Only use Outlook if you are locked into Microsoft 365, since Spark is a better pick for most personal email needs.
Spark gives you a faster and more organized email experience than Gmail, which feels cluttered and slow for anyone who gets a lot of email.
Only use Gmail if you just need the default that comes with Google Workspace, but Spark is the clear pick if you want your inbox to feel more manageable.
Notion Mail gives you a nicer interface and lets you pull in Notion docs while emailing, which Spark does not.
Only use Spark if you want something free and a bit faster than Gmail or Outlook, but Notion Mail feels nicer to use day to day.

Spark is a better option if you're an individual or team looking to streamline email. Hey requires you to use their own domain @hey.com, making it more isolated rather than just using Spark on top of Gmail or Outlook.
If email delegation or team collaboration is the main reason you're looking for an email client, we'd say Missive might be the best to check out.

The reason? Missive allows you to have a central inbox for any business-related messages (beyond email) — from social media apps, to SMS, to WhatsApp. Missive's collaborative features are also more established than Spark's, so you'll get a more reliable experience.
Most people compare Spark to Superhuman and
want
to hear that Spark is better because there is a free tier... but we're here to say that Superhuman is far superior and worth the investment (we've been paying customers for 6+ years!).
We do think that Spark
is
better than Gmail/Outlook and is a good option if you're an individual on a budget, but if a professional and you're looking for "the best" email experience, Superhuman takes the cake.
Superhuman vs Spark: A Detailed Comparison

When comparing Spark vs Superhuman, most folks fixate on the price difference. Spark has a free version (or is $60 per year on the paid tier), while Superhuman comes in at $30 per month, so you may be wondering,
is Superhuman really worth the extra cost
Well, after using and being paying customers of Superhuman for 6+ years, we're here to tell you that yes, it is 😄
The truth of the matter is that while Spark and Superhuman seem to have similar features on the surface,
Superhuman goes the extra mile and puts extra TLC into each feature
. Plus Superhuman is built more for professional use, while Spark is something that just any who wants a better email experience than Gmail or Outlook could use.
Here are some examples of how Spark and Superhuman differ ⤵️
Design
While Spark's interface is miles better than Gmail or Outlook,
it still feels—well—like email.
Superhuman on the other hand gives your entire email app a facelift with its
beautiful minimal design, consistent colors, fonts, and automatic resizing of emails

to make them readable no matter your screen size. While Spark tries to do a bit of this, it's clear that less TLC went into the interface. For instance, in Spark, emails stretch with your screen, making them more difficult to read.
In Spark, emails stretch with your screen, making them more difficult to read.

Superhuman sizes your emails perfectly no matter your screen size. Also, no bright colors, and just a beautiful minimal email view. Also take note of the AI summary of the email up top! This automatically happens with each email.
Email Snoozing
With Spark, you can snooze emails, but the
ability to write in natural language is nowhere near Superhuman's. For instance, in Superhuman, you can snooze until "Next Wednesday at 9 a.m. in Australia".
Superhuman automatically does the time conversion for you
and
allows you to fully write in natural language
, while with Spark, you really can only choose from the predetermined options like tomorrow, or a specific date.
We use natural language multiple times a day when snoozing our emails in Superhuman and can't imagine having guardrails with limited options.
AI Email Features
Spark + AI and Superhuman AI helps you summarize and respond to email messages faster.
With Superhuman, we see it going just one level deeper, training the AI to "write in your tone" based on your previous emails
. While we don't use AI a ton for writing emails, the "write in my tone" feature in Superhuman is the one we tend to lean on the most.

It also seems that Superhuman takes in far more context from the entire conversation than Spark does, but that also makes sense because there is a cost associated with this, and since Superhuman is a premium product, they can spend more on AI tokens (giving the prompt more context).
You can summarize emails with Spark + AI on the paid tier. Superhuman does this AI summary automatically for you.
Simply write a prompt and allow Spark + AI to generate a response. You can ask it to change the tone of your email, or shorten/expand it.
Spark Email AI Response With Prompt
With Superhuman, write down your thoughts and ask it to
"rewrite in your voice" which is learns from previous emails 🤯
. I think it's important to still sound "like you" in emails, and I found that Superhuman even pulls out emoji's that I typically use when using this feature!
Smart Inbox
While
Spark Mail
categorizes your emails based on people, newsletters, and notifications
, Superhuman allows you to go deeper and categorize emails based on your own preferences into Split Inbox (e.g. emails with a similar subject type, or senders from a specific domain).
We use the Split Inbox feature in Superhuman to help us triage similar emails together, reducing the need to context switch
Notifications and Newsletters are automatically grouped together in Spark, but adding further customization isn't possible.
Superhuman beautifully splits your inbox for you based on how you customize it.
Unified Inbox
Spark has a unified inbox

, meaning that all of your emails can technically come into one inbox and some people tend to *love* this. It can be helpful if you have multiple work emails for example, and you want them all to come into one inbox, but you still want to maintain the separate emails.

With Superhuman, while you don't have a unified inbox, you can simply jump to another inbox using the keyboard shortcut CTRL 1, 2, 3, etc. On the Superhuman mobile app, swiping through accounts is as easy as clicking your profile icon.
We actually prefer having the inboxes separated as mentally,
checking work emails vs personal emails require different mental effort
, so even if Superhuman offered this feature, we'd have to think twice before turning it on 🤔.
Final Verdict
Overall, if you're a professional who wants the
best email client, claiming the
Superhuman Mail free trial
is probably worth it alongside Spark
, to really compare the two tools and see which one you get the most out of.
For us, when it comes to emails for work and improving efficiency, there is a clear winner of the two, with Superhuman taking the cake.

items.name: Missive
items.description: Team inbox and chat tool that empowers teams to collaborate around email, SMS & social media messaging apps.

children.text: Plain gives you a simple single inbox and tight integrations with Slack and Linear, while Missive focuses on handling emails and messages from multiple platforms with easy delegation and organized team discussions.
Only use Plain if you care most about an API-first help desk and software tool integrations; stick with Missive for smoother team email and message collaboration.

If your team needs deep integrations with Slack, Linear, and Stripe, and you're a product-led or engineering-heavy company where support and engineering are tightly connected, Plain is built for you. Its single inbox and API-first setup make connecting your support workflow to your dev tools seamless, especially if you're already living in Slack and Linear. But if you want traditional shared inbox workflows (like handling contact@, billing@, admin@ addresses) or need something for non-technical support staff, Plain will feel limiting.

Missive, on the other hand, shines when you need to manage and delegate messages from multiple channels (email, SMS, WhatsApp, social media) in one place. It's especially strong for small businesses or teams where inbox delegation, assignment, and lightweight automation matter. The ability to assign emails, set rules, and collaborate directly in the inbox makes it great for businesses handling lots of external messages across platforms, without needing the engineering focus or tool integration that Plain offers.

So if your priority is tight integration with dev tools and you're a technical product team, go with Plain. If you care more about multi-channel communication, inbox delegation, and team collaboration across all kinds of messages, Missive is the clear pick.

app.name: Missive
app.description: Team inbox and chat tool that empowers teams to collaborate around email, SMS & social media messaging apps.

children.text: Missive lets your team manage and collaborate on emails and messages from different platforms in one place, while Inbox Zero focuses on labeling, organizing, and bulk unsubscribing from emails but needs Google Workspace or Microsoft.
Only use Inbox Zero if you just want to clean up and organize your inbox, otherwise Missive is the easier pick for team email collaboration.

Missive is built for teams who need to handle emails and messages from multiple platforms together, all in one place. The standout is its ability to let you assign emails to teammates, create auto-routing rules, and collaborate directly inside threads without switching tools. If you run a small business or manage a team where inbox delegation and internal discussion around messages are constant, Missive is the clear pick. It keeps everything organized and lets you control who sees what, which is something you just don't get with most email tools.

Inbox Zero is all about helping you personally organize your Gmail or Outlook inbox with AI, mainly by bulk unsubscribing from newsletters and automating labeling or forwarding. It's open source and great if your main headache is sorting and cleaning up your own inbox, not team collaboration.

If your priority is team-based email delegation, assignment, and collaboration across channels, Missive is miles ahead. If you just want to clean up your own inbox with smart AI sorting and don't care about team workflows, Inbox Zero is better suited for you. For anything beyond solo inbox management, Missive is the obvious choice.

Missive keeps team email and messages organized from multiple platforms and lets you delegate inboxes easily, while Intercom only makes sense if you have a big team ready for constant live chat and can handle high costs.
Only use Intercom if you really need nonstop live chat and have the staff for it; otherwise, Missive is the practical pick for most teams.

If you want to keep all your team's emails, chats, and social DMs in one place and easily assign them to different people, Missive is miles ahead. It makes delegating, collaborating, and triaging across channels simple, especially for small businesses juggling lots of inboxes or anyone who wants to avoid the chaos of switching between apps. You can even set up rules to automatically route messages, and there's built-in team chat on every thread so nothing gets lost.

Intercom is only worth considering if you have a full-time support team dedicated to live chat and can handle the pressure of responding instantly to every message. For small teams, it turns into a constant stressor and the costs skyrocket fast, especially as your contacts grow. It's not built for running all your team's communications or for flexible delegation.

If you're a small business or a team that needs to collaborate on messages from everywhere, Missive is the clear choice. Intercom only makes sense if you're running a big live chat operation with staff to match. For most teams, Missive actually solves the real communication headaches.
Help Scout makes your customers feel like people instead of ticket numbers, unlike Missive which is focused on team email collaboration.
Only use Missive if your main need is organizing team discussions and managing messages from several platforms in one inbox.

If you care most about keeping customer interactions feeling personal and not like tickets in a queue, Help Scout nails it. It's built for teams that put customer experience first, especially small businesses and startups that want to make every customer feel heard. The whole product is designed to avoid the usual support-bot runaround, so people always get a thoughtful, human touch. If your team's identity is wrapped up in delivering standout customer service, this is the better pick.

Missive, on the other hand, is all about bringing together every message from everywhere, email, SMS, WhatsApp, social DMs, into one place and making it easy for your team to divvy up the work. If you're juggling lots of channels and need to delegate messages fast without losing control, Missive is strong, especially for teams with heavy inbox collaboration. But its approach is more about internal efficiency and less about making customers feel special.

So if your top priority is treating every customer like an individual and giving them a real human touch, go with Help Scout. If you just need to wrangle a chaotic, multi-channel inbox and keep your team on top of everything, Missive is the practical choice. But for customer-centric support that feels personal, Help Scout stands out.
Gorgias handles eCommerce support best by connecting deeply with tools like Shopify, while Missive focuses on team email and message collaboration across platforms.
Only use Gorgias if you need help desk features tied to eCommerce tools; stick with Missive if your team needs to manage shared inboxes and messages together.

If your team runs an eCommerce store, Gorgias is the clear pick because it's built specifically for that world. You get deep Shopify integration, instant access to customer order history right in the help desk, and automations that handle the flood of repetitive support tickets eCommerce brands deal with every day. This setup directly cuts response times and keeps your team from getting buried.

Missive, on the other hand, shines if your main struggle is wrangling messages from email, WhatsApp, SMS, and social DMs into a single inbox for a small business. It's built for teams that need to delegate emails and collaborate across channels, but it doesn't have the same specialized tools or integrations for eCommerce.

If you're supporting an online store and care about tight Shopify integration and automating order-related support, Gorgias is built for exactly that. If your business is more about managing lots of channels and shared inboxes without the eCommerce focus, Missive is the better fit. For eCommerce help desks, though, Gorgias is the obvious choice.

Front combines email, social, and chat into one inbox, which helps if you really rely on all those channels for support, while Missive focuses on keeping team email and message collaboration organized and easy to delegate.
Only use Front if your support depends on juggling social and chat with email; choose Missive if you mostly need smooth team email management.

Missive handles multi-channel inbox consolidation with more flexibility for teams who need to delegate, assign, and collaborate on messages from different platforms. Its standout is letting you assign emails to specific teammates and set up automation rules based on sender or topic, so things like sales or invoices go straight to the right person. The built-in team chat keeps all your discussions tied to the message, cutting down on back-and-forth in other apps.

Front only makes sense if your support genuinely runs through multiple social and chat channels. If your workflow is mostly email or you're not ready to commit to making Front your main inbox, you won't see the full benefit. For teams that just want to occasionally pull in a few DMs from social, Front adds more complexity than it solves and risks fragmenting your support process further.

If you're a business owner or small team that needs to actually distribute and manage messages across your team, Missive is the better pick. It gives you real delegation, automation, and team collaboration in one place. Front is only worth it if you absolutely live in multi-channel support and are ready to centralize everything around its shared inbox. Otherwise, Missive is the clear choice for multi-channel team communication.
Missive lets your team manage and collaborate on emails and messages from multiple platforms in one organized place, while Tatem is minimal but appears to be abandoned and may not keep working.
Only use Tatem if you are fine with no updates or support; stick with Missive for teamwork and ongoing reliability.
Missive lets teams manage and collaborate on emails and messages from different platforms in one organized place, while Skiff is no longer available since it was shut down after being acquired.
Only use Missive, since Skiff cannot be used at all.

Missive actually works for team email management and collaboration, while Skiff is no longer available since it was acquired and shut down. If you need something your team can use right now to handle shared inboxes, delegate messages, and keep all your communications in one place, Missive is the only real option.
There's just nothing to compare here for active use. Skiff doesn't exist anymore, so if you want a working email tool, go with Missive.
Missive keeps team email and message collaboration organized in one place, while Outlook sticks to standard email without team-focused features.
Only use Outlook if you are locked into Microsoft 365, but Missive is the clear pick for any team that needs shared inbox management.
Missive lets teams manage and collaborate on emails and messages from different platforms in one spot, while Notion Mail is just a Gmail wrapper with a Notion look and a few extras.
Only use Notion Mail if you really want a Gmail experience with Notion-style views, but choose Missive for actual team collaboration and inbox delegation.
